EXPOSÉ 0
SKETCH OF A PROGRAM FOR A THEORY OF INTERSECTIONS ON GENERAL SCHEMESby A. Grothendieck

The present exposé is of an introductory nature, and reading it is not logically indispensable for the study of the Seminar. It is addressed more particularly to readers familiar with the provisional version of the Riemann-Roch theorem, as it is set out in the report of Borel-Serre \([2]\) or in the report of Grothendieck already mentioned in the Introduction (cited \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) in what follows), which is reproduced as an appendix at the end of the present exposé.

1. Let us recall the Riemann-Roch formula for a proper morphism \[f\colon X \to Y\] of schemes quasi-projective and smooth over a field \(k\), and a coherent sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), whose class in the group \(K(X)\) of classes of coherent sheaves on \(X\) is denoted by \(c\ell(F)\): \[\operatorname{Todd}(T_Y)\, \operatorname{ch}_Y(f_*(c\ell(F))) = f_*(\operatorname{Todd}(T_X)\, \operatorname{ch}_X(F)) \tag{1.1}\] This formula is valid in \(A(Y)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Q}\), where \(A(Y)\) is the Chow ring of \(Y\), the \(f_*\) of the right-hand side being deduced by tensoring with \(\mathbb{Q}\) from the "direct image of cycles" homomorphism \[f_*\colon A(X) \to A(Y) ,\] that of the left-hand side being the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(F\) relative to \(f\): \[f_*(c\ell(F)) = \sum_i (-1)^i c\ell(R^i f_*(F)) ;\] \(\operatorname{ch}_X\), \(\operatorname{ch}_Y\) denote the Chern characters on \(X\) resp. on \(Y\), and \(T_X,T_Y\) the tangent bundles of \(X\) resp. of \(Y\). As is well known, \(\operatorname{Todd}(-)\) and \(\operatorname{ch}(-)\) are universal polynomials with coefficients in \(\mathbb{Q}\) in the Chern classes of the argument. The constant term of \(\operatorname{Todd}(-)\) being \(1\), it is an invertible element for every value of the argument, so that formula (1.1) takes, after multiplication by \(\operatorname{Todd}(T_Y)^{-1}\), the equivalent form, more convenient for our purpose \[\operatorname{ch}_Y(f_*(c\ell(F))) = f_*(\operatorname{Todd}(T_f)\cdot\operatorname{ch}_X(F)) , \tag{1.2}\] where we have set \[T_f = T_X - f^*(T_Y) \in K(X) , \tag{1.3}\] so that \(T_f\) plays the role of a virtual relative tangent bundle of \(X\) over \(Y\). In the case where the morphism \(f\) is smooth (i.e. with tangent map everywhere surjective), we have simply \[T_f = T_{X/Y} \quad \text{(fibré tangent le long des fibres)} ,\] while in the case where \(f\colon X \to Y\) is an immersion, one finds \[T_f = -\check{N}_{X/Y} ,\] where \(\check{N}_{X/Y}\) denotes the normal sheaf of \(X\) in \(Y\).
One of the principal aims of the Seminar is to generalize (1.2) simultaneously in two directions:

1. a) To get rid of the hypothesis of the existence of a base field \(k\).
  2. b) To replace the regularity hypotheses on \(Y,X\) by a hypothesis of "local regularity" on \(f\).
Finally, along the way we shall also concern ourselves with the problem:
1. c) To eliminate the quasi-projectivity hypotheses which, in the absence of a base field, are expressed by the existence of ample invertible Modules on \(X\) and on \(Y\).
2. Let us first examine generalization a), while however keeping the hypotheses of regularity and of existence of ample invertible Modules on \(X\) and on \(Y\).

The definition of \(K(X),K(Y)\) and of the homomorphism \(f_*\colon K(X) \to K(Y)\) then offers no new problem, thanks to the fact that \(X\) and \(Y\) are regular. The most natural route to giving a meaning to (1.2) thus seems to consist in the definition of Chow rings \(A(X),A(Y)\) and of a homomorphism of groups \[f_*\colon A(X) \to A(Y) ,\] in the establishment of a theory of Chern classes, providing maps \[c_i\colon K(X) \to A(X) ,\] and likewise for \(Y\), and finally in the description of a virtual relative tangent bundle element \[T_f \in K(X) .\]

2.1 As for the latter, a definition presents itself fairly obviously. One uses the fact that the morphism \(f\), thanks to the hypothesis of the existence of an ample invertible Module on \(X\) and on \(Y\), can be factored as \[X \xrightarrow{\ i\ } X' \xrightarrow{\ f'\ } Y , \tag{2.1}\] where \(i\) is a closed immersion, and \(f'\) a smooth morphism; for example one may take \(X' = \mathbb{P}^r_Y\) for suitable \(r\). One then sets \[\check{T}_f = c\ell(\Omega^1_{X'/Y}) - c\ell(N_{X/X'}) , \tag{2.2}\] where \(\Omega^1_{X'/Y}\) is the locally free Module of relative \(1\)-differentials of \(X'\) over \(Y\) (or relative cotangent Module of \(X'\) over \(Y\)), and \(N_{X/X'}\) the conormal Module of \(X\) in \(X'\), equal by definition to \(J/J^2\), where \(J\) is the Ideal on \(X'\) defining the closed subscheme \(X\); as \(X\) and \(X'\) are regular, one concludes moreover that \(N_{X/X'}\) is also locally free. Finally \(\check{T}_f\) denotes the dual of \(T_f\). One verifies without difficulty (Exp VIII) that the element \(T_f\) defined by (2.1) does not depend on the chosen factorization of \(f\).

2.2 As for a definition, under the conditions envisaged, of a Chow ring and of a corresponding theory of Chern classes, although it is plausible that such a theory should admit a development on the model of the one already known for smooth quasi-projective algebraic schemes, it seems that this work has not been done at the present time, and a fortiori has not been undertaken in the Seminar. One introduces instead another ring, which will play a role analogous to that of the Chow ring, and whose tensor product with \(\mathbb{Q}\) is indeed isomorphic to \(A(X)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Q}\) in the case where \(X\) is quasi-projective and smooth over a field \(k\). This ring is the graded ring associated to \(K(X)\), for a suitable filtration of this ring. A first natural filtration of \(K(X)\), which is the one envisaged in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\), is the "topological filtration" by the codimension of the support of coherent sheaves, \(\operatorname{Filt}^i_{\mathrm{top}} K(X)\) being generated by the \(c\ell(F)\) with \(F\) coherent and \(\operatorname{codim}(\operatorname{supp} F) \geq i\). A first question is that of the compatibility of this filtration with the ring structure, a question resolved in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\), in the case \(X\) smooth over a field \(k\), thanks to Chow's "moving lemma". This point granted, one thus finds an associated graded ring, which we shall denote here \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\). In \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) one defines a surjective ring homomorphism \[\delta\colon A(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X) ,\] (transforming the class of a prime cycle \(Z\) into \(c\ell(\mathcal{O}_Z)\),) whose kernel turns out (Exp. XIV 4.2) to be a torsion subgroup. Moreover, one shows in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) how the images in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) of the Chern classes \(c_i(F)\) can be computed directly in terms of \(c\ell(F) \in K(X)\) and of the exterior power operations \(\lambda^i\) in \(K(X)\).

This shows that, for \(X\) a regular noetherian scheme equipped with an ample invertible Module, the ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) is a convenient substitute for the hypothetical Chow ring \(A(X)\); it moreover has the advantage over the latter that it is directly in this ring that certain computations of \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) are carried out, leading to important formulas which even for \(X\) projective and smooth over a field are not established at the present time in \(A(X)\) itself (Exp. XIV, 4.3 and 4.4). To justify this point of view entirely, one would however have to solve the problem pointed out above of the compatibility of the topological filtration of \(K(X)\) with its ring structure, a problem which seems essentially tied to Chow's "moving lemma", which itself is the essential technical ingredient of the theory of the Chow ring and which we have claimed to be able to avoid.

2.3 To avoid this problem, one equips \(K(X)\) with a second filtration, finer than the topological filtration, and which can be described in purely algebraic terms starting from the structure of augmented \(\lambda\)-ring on \(K(X)\). The definition of this filtration is suggested by the expression given in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) for the Chern classes of a \(x \in K(X)\) in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), \[c_i(x) = \gamma^i(x - \varepsilon(x)) \bmod \operatorname{Filt}^{i+1}_{\mathrm{top}} K(X) , \tag{2.3}\] where \(\gamma^i\) denotes a certain linear combination of the \(\lambda^j\) \((j \leq i)\) made explicit in loc. cit., \(\varepsilon\) being the augmentation. One shows that \(\gamma^i(x - \varepsilon(x))\) is indeed of topological filtration \(\geq i\), which gives a meaning to formula (2.3). One then defines the \(\lambda\)-filtration of \(K(X)\) by the ideals \(\operatorname{Filt}^i K(X)\), where \(\operatorname{Filt}^i K(X)\) is formed by the sums of expressions of the form \[\gamma^{i_1}(x_1 - \varepsilon(x_1)) \cdots \gamma^{i_k}(x_k - \varepsilon(x_k)) , \quad i_1 + \cdots + i_k \geq i .\] The graded ring associated to this filtration will be denoted \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), and it is this ring which will replace the Chow ring of \(X\) in the Seminar. One can moreover show that in the case envisaged here the canonical homomorphism \[\operatorname{Gr}'(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X) \tag{2.4}\] is an isomorphism modulo torsion, which justifies the point of view according to which this ring tensored with \(\mathbb{Q}\) can play in every respect the same role as the Chow ring tensored with \(\mathbb{Q}\). On the other hand, we have done exactly what was needed for formula (2.3) (where one replaces \(\operatorname{Filt}^i_{\mathrm{top}}\) by \(\operatorname{Filt}^i\)) to define a theory of Chern classes with values in \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), from which the Chern classes in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) are deduced by means of the homomorphism (2.4). One shows (Exp.V), thanks to the fact (Exp.VI) that \(K(X)\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring which is special in the sense of \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) (i.e. a \(\lambda\)-ring in the sense of Exp.V), that this notion of Chern class satisfies all the usual formal properties, reviewed in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\).

2.4 One will note a very appreciable advantage of the definition of \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) over that of \(A(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), namely that it keeps a meaning for an absolutely arbitrary scheme \(X\), and more generally for an arbitrary locally ringed topos \(X\), by then taking for \(K(X)\) the group of classes constructed with the locally free Modules on \(X\), which is again an augmented \(\lambda\)-ring, hence equipped with a corresponding filtration, allowing one to define an associated graded ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\). As the price of this generality, one must however remark that when one is not willing to neglect torsion phenomena, i.e. to tensor with \(\mathbb{Q}\), the properties of \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) are less satisfactory in various respects than those of its two competitors (Exp.XIV n° 4). Thus, assuming again that \(f\colon X \to Y\) is a proper morphism of regular noetherian schemes equipped with ample invertible Modules, it is not true in general that the homomorphism \(f_*\colon K(X) \to K(Y)\) maps \(\operatorname{Filt}^i K(X)\) into \(\operatorname{Filt}^{i-d} K(Y)\), where \(d\) is the relative dimension of \(X\) over \(Y\), and consequently defines, by passage to the associated graded rings, a homomorphism of degree \(-d\) from \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) to \(\operatorname{Gr}(Y)\). However, one can prove (Exp.VII) that this is the case provided one tensors with \(\mathbb{Q}\), so that one obtains a homomorphism of degree \(-d\) \[f_*\colon \operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb{Q}} \to \operatorname{Gr}'(Y)_{\mathbb{Q}} , \tag{2.5}\] playing the role of the "direct image of cycles" homomorphism \(A(X) \to A(Y)\).

This granted, we see then that we are in possession of all the structural elements needed to give a meaning to formula (1.2) when one does not postulate the existence of a base field, \(f\colon X \to Y\) being a proper morphism of regular noetherian schemes admitting ample invertible Modules.

3. Let us now show how one can still give a meaning to formula (1.2) when one abandons the regularity hypothesis made on \(X,Y\), replacing it by a hypothesis of a local nature on the morphism \(f\), which we shall make precise.

3.1 A first difficulty comes from the fact that the observation which had served as the starting point of the theory \([\mathrm{RRR}]\), namely that the group \(K(X)\) of classes of sheaves on \(X\) is the same, whether one defines it in terms of coherent sheaves or in terms of locally free sheaves, is tied in an essential way to the regularity hypothesis on \(X\). In the case where \(X\) is a general scheme (say, for simplicity), it is appropriate to distinguish between these two groups, which we shall denote respectively \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) and \(K'(X)\), the first having a covariant character in \(X\) for proper morphisms, the second a contravariant character in \(X\) for arbitrary morphisms. One will note that \(K'(X)\) is naturally equipped with a ring structure (and even with an augmented \(\lambda\)-ring structure), whereas on \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) there is in general no longer a ring structure, but only a module structure over \(K'(X)\). The homomorphism \(x \mapsto x\cdot c\ell(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (where \(c\ell(F)\) denotes the class of \(F\) in \(K_{\bullet}(X)\)) from \(K'(X)\) to \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) is no longer an isomorphism in general. For our formulation of the generalized Riemann-Roch theorem, we shall work exclusively with \(K'(X), K'(Y)\), to the exclusion of \(K_{\bullet}(X), K_{\bullet}(Y)\). As we pointed out in 2.3., these augmented \(\lambda\)-rings, equipped with their natural filtration, give rise to the graded rings \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X), \operatorname{Gr}'(Y)\), and to "Chern class" homomorphisms \[c_i\colon K'(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}'(X) ,\] and likewise for \(Y\). The difficulty comes however in the definition of a direct image homomorphism \[f_*\colon K'(X) \to K'(Y) , \tag{3.1}\] which can no longer be defined by the naive formula \[f_*(c\ell(F)) = \sum_i (-1)^i c\ell(R^i f_*(F)) ,\] for even if \(F\) is locally free, the Modules \(R^i f_*(F)\) are in general no longer locally free, nor even of finite tor-dimension, and the preceding formula loses its meaning. A solution to this difficulty is provided by the point of view of derived categories. Indeed, whereas the \(R^i f_*(F)\) are in general "bad" sheaves, so to speak, to which one could not associate a class in \(K'(Y)\), nevertheless the complex \(Rf_*(F)\) on \(Y\), the direct image of \(F\) in the sense of derived categories, whose \(R^i f_*(F)\) are the cohomology sheaves, tends to be excellent. More precisely, suppose that \(F\) is of finite tor-dimension over \(\mathcal{O}_Y\), which will be the case if \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is of finite tor-dimension over \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) and if moreover \(F\) is locally free. Then one shows (Exp. III) that the complex \(Rf_*(F)\) is "perfect", by which is meant here: with coherent cohomology sheaves (which was already known in any case) and of finite global tor-dimension; or again: on every affine open \(U\) of \(Y\), this complex is isomorphic, in the derived category \(D(U)\), to a complex with bounded degrees and locally free of finite type in each degree. From the point of view of Homological Algebra, the perfect complexes are "as good" as locally free Modules, of which they constitute the natural generalization. They moreover form a "triangulated subcategory" of the derived category \(D(Y)\), i.e. a subcategory stable under cones (or "mapping cylinders") of morphisms. On the other hand, to every triangulated category \(C\) one associates a group \(K(C)\) of classes, a natural variant of the construction of the group of classes of an abelian category, by taking the free group generated by the objects of \(C\), divided by the relations \[c\ell(K) - c\ell(K') - c\ell(K'')\] for every distinguished triangle \[\xymatrix@R=1em@C=1em{ & K'' \ar[dl] & \\ K' \ar[rr] & & K \ar[ul] }\] What then replaces the fundamental observation of \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) in the present case is the fact that, when the scheme \(Y\) admits an ample invertible Module, every perfect complex \(L_{\bullet}\) on \(Y\) is globally (and not only locally) isomorphic in \(D(Y)\) to a complex \(L'\) with bounded degrees and locally free of finite type in each degree (Exp II); whence it follows easily (Exp.IV) that the canonical homomorphism \[K'(X) \to K(\operatorname{Parf}(Y)) ,\] where \(\operatorname{Parf}(Y)\) is the triangulated category of perfect complexes on \(Y\), is an isomorphism. Hence to every perfect complex \(L_{\bullet}\) on \(Y\) one can associate a class \[c\ell(L_{\bullet}) \in K'(Y) ,\] which is moreover none other than \[c\ell(L_{\bullet}) = \sum_i (-1)^i c\ell(L'_i)\] where \(L'\) is as above, so that the \(L'_i\) are locally free Modules on \(Y\) whose classes one can take in \(K'(Y)\).

This granted, one defines a homomorphism (3.1) whenever \(f\colon X \to Y\) is a proper morphism of finite tor-dimension of noetherian schemes such that \(Y\) admits an ample invertible Module, by simply setting \[f_*(c\ell(F)) = c\ell(Rf_*(F)) \tag{3.2}\] for every locally free Module \(F\) on \(X\) (so that \(Rf_*(F)\) is indeed a perfect complex on \(Y\), and the right-hand side is defined). When \(X\) also admits an ample invertible Module, then \(K'(X)\) can likewise be interpreted as \(K(\operatorname{Parf}(X))\), and the homomorphism (3.1) is none other than the one which is associated, by functoriality, to the exact functor of triangulated categories induced by \[Rf_*\colon \operatorname{Parf}(X) \to \operatorname{Parf}(Y) .\]

3.2 To give a meaning to (1.2), it still remains to make on \(f\) a hypothesis allowing one to give a meaning to the homomorphism (2.5) \[f_*\colon \operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb{Q}} \to \operatorname{Gr}'(Y)_{\mathbb{Q}} , \tag{3.3}\] i.e. ensuring that the homomorphism (3.1) just defined is (modulo a shift and modulo torsion) compatible with the filtrations of these rings; and finally to define also a "virtual relative tangent bundle" element \[T_f \in K'(X) .\] For the latter, taking up again the definition proposed in 2.1, one sees that it is appropriate to make a hypothesis ensuring that (with the notations of loc. cit.) the conormal Module \(N_{X/X'}\) is locally free. The most natural hypothesis to this effect is that \(i\) be a "regular immersion", i.e. that it identify \(X\) with a subscheme of \(X'\) defined by an Ideal which locally can be defined by an \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\)-regular sequence of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\). This hypothesis, of a purely local nature, does not in fact depend on the choice of the factorization of \(f\) into an immersion followed by a smooth morphism (Exp. VIII), and as above, the same holds for the class \(T_f\) defined by formula (2.1). When the hypothesis just envisaged is satisfied, one says that the morphism \(f\) is a "local complete intersection" morphism(∗). Such a morphism is moreover locally of finite tor-dimension.

If \(f\) is a local complete intersection morphism, one introduces a natural notion of "virtual relative dimension" \(d(f)\) by the formula \[d(f)(x) = \dim_x(f'^{-1}f(x)) - \operatorname{codim}_x(X,X') .\] It is an integer-valued function, locally constant in the point \(x \in X\), hence constant if \(X\) is connected, which moreover does not depend either on the choice of the factorization (2.1). When this virtual relative dimension \(d\) is constant, \(f\) is proper, and \(X\) and \(Y\) admit ample invertible Modules, one can show (Exp. VIII), although this is far from being a trivial result, that we have \[f_*(\operatorname{Filt}^i K'(X)_{\mathbb{Q}}) \subseteq \operatorname{Filt}^{i-d} K'(Y)_{\mathbb{Q}} , \tag{3.4}\] whence a homomorphism (3.3) of degree \(-d\).

3.3 We have therefore gathered all the structural elements needed to give a meaning to formula (1.2) in the case of a proper local complete intersection morphism of noetherian schemes \(X,Y\) both admitting ample invertible Modules. With a little extra care, this formulation moreover keeps a meaning independently of any noetherian hypothesis.

In this general form, this Riemann-Roch formula will be proved in Exp. VIII. The reader will observe that the proof given in this Seminar combines elements of the two proofs which appear in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) and which were discussed in the Introduction. The necessity of resorting to the methods of the first of these proofs, involving more \(\lambda\)-formalism than the second (followed in Borel-Serre), is due to the necessity of a proof of the inclusion (3.4), a question which did not arise in the more restricted setting of \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) and Borel-Serre \([2]\), where one could work with the Chow ring or the ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}\). From the second proof, we retain the introduction of the blown-up scheme of \(Y\) along \(X\) in the case where \(f\) is an immersion. This technical artifice will no doubt disappear, and the proof of the case of an immersion will extend to the case where one no longer postulates the existence of an ample invertible Module on \(Y\), once the question raised in Exp. XIV n° 1 on the introduction of exterior power operations in the derived category \(D^-(Y)\) is resolved.
4. We must finally give some indications on the way in which one can still give a meaning to (1.2) in the absence of a hypothesis of existence of ample invertible Modules on \(X\) and \(Y\).

4.1 Let us first note that for a scheme \(X\) (or more generally, an arbitrary locally ringed topos), it seems beyond doubt that the "good" invariant which must replace the ring \(K(X)\) of \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) is not the ring \(K'(X)\) considered previously, defined in terms of locally free Modules on \(X\), but rather the group \(K(\operatorname{Parf}(X))\), envisaged in 3.1., defined in terms of perfect complexes on \(X\), and which we shall distinguish from the "naive" group of 3.1, which we shall denote \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\). This point of view is imposed by the necessity of defining a direct image homomorphism (3.1) for a morphism \(f\colon X \to Y\) which is proper and of finite tor-dimension, of noetherian schemes say (the non-noetherian case requiring somewhat stronger local finiteness properties on \(f\), cf. Exp. III). With the definition of \(K'(X)\) now adopted, this homomorphism \(f_*\) is indeed defined in a trivial way by formula (3.2), where \(F\) is an arbitrary perfect complex on \(X\), which implies that \(Rf_*(F)\) is also perfect.

4.2 This new definition of \(K'(X)\) raises however a new problem of homological algebra, which is that of a definition of a \(\lambda\)-structure on \(K'(X)\), compatible with the natural ring structure coming from the tensor product in the derived category. (NB If \(L_{\bullet}\) and \(L'\) are two perfect complexes, the same holds for their total tensor product \(L_{\bullet} \otimes L'\) ). More precisely, one would need to define "exterior power" operations in the derived category \(D^-(X)\), transforming perfect complexes into perfect complexes. This question has not yet been cleared up at the present time, and as was said in the Introduction, it constitutes the most serious gap in the Seminar. Once this question is resolved, and proceeding as in 2.3, one finds a natural filtration on \(K'(X)\), allowing one to form the associated graded ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), which will play the role of the Chow ring, and to define a theory of Chern classes \[c_i\colon K'(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}'(X)\]

4.3 To give a meaning to (1.2) for a proper local complete intersection morphism \(f\colon X \to Y\) of noetherian schemes, one must further include in the statement of this formula the non-trivial inclusions (3.4) (where \(d\) is the virtual relative dimension) in order to allow one to define (3.3), and finally to define the virtual relative tangent class \[T_f \in K'(X)\] When \(f\) admits a factorization (2.1) through an immersion into a smooth relative scheme, the definition of \(T_f\) offers no new difficulty. One can moreover reformulate it in this case, in a manner more in keeping with the "derived categories" spirit, by considering the canonical homomorphism \[N_{X/X'} \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \Omega^1_{X'/Y} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X'}} \mathcal{O}_X \tag{4.1}\] induced by the exterior differential \[d_{X'/X}\colon J \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{X'} \to \Omega^1_{X'/Y} ,\] and the chain complex \[L^{X/Y}_{\bullet} \in \operatorname{Ob} D(X) \tag{4.2}\] defined thanks to (4.1) "by extending by zeros", a complex equal in degree \(1\) resp. \(0\) to the source resp. the target of the homomorphism (4.1). Formula (2.2) then takes the more congenial form \[\check{T}_f = c\ell(L^{X/Y}_{\bullet}) , \tag{4.3}\] a formula which has a meaning in \(K'(X)\) when \(f\) is a local complete intersection morphism, since in this case the complex \(L^{X/Y}_{\bullet}\) is manifestly perfect. The assertion of invariance of \(T_f\) with respect to the chosen factorization of \(f\) can then easily be sharpened into an assertion of invariance for the complex \(L^{X/Y}_{\bullet}\): modulo canonical isomorphism in the derived category \(D(X)\), this complex is likewise independent of the chosen factorization (Exp. VIII).

4.4 When \(f\colon X \to Y\) is an arbitrary morphism of schemes, one can still construct a canonical object \(L^{X/Y}_{\bullet}\) of the derived category \(D(X)\), defined up to unique isomorphism, which on each affine open of \(X\) lying over an affine open of \(Y\) is a complex constructed according to the principle set out in the preceding paragraph (where it is however appropriate in the general case to replace "smooth" by "formally smooth", so that every algebra \(B\) over a ring \(A\) appears as a quotient of a formally smooth algebra, for example a polynomial algebra). For such a definition it is not possible to proceed by simple "gluing" from the affine constructions, for the objects of the derived category \(D(X)\) are of an essentially non-gluable nature. Here is a general construction of \(L^{X/Y}_{\bullet}\), valid more generally for any morphism \(f\colon X \to Y\) of (commutatively) ringed topoi: Let \(B = \mathcal{O}_X, A = f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_Y)\), so that \(A\) is a Ring on the space \(X\), and \(B\) an \(A\)-Algebra. Let \(T \to B\) be a homomorphism of sheaves of sets which generates \(B\) as an \(A\)-Algebra, i.e. such that the corresponding homomorphism of \(A\)-Algebras \[\phi\colon C = A[T] \to B\] is an epimorphism of sheaves of sets, where \(A[T]\) denotes the free commutative \(A\)-Algebra generated by \(T\) (i.e. the sheaf associated to the presheaf \(U \mapsto A(U)[T(U)]\) of free commutative \(A\)-Algebras generated by \(T(U)\) ). Let \(J\) be the kernel of \(\phi\), and let us consider as in 4.3 the chain complex of length \(1\) defined by the natural homomorphism \[J/J^2 \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \Omega^1_{C/A} \otimes_C B .\] It is not difficult to see that, up to canonical isomorphism in the category \(D(X)\), the complex thus constructed is independent of the choice of \(T\) and \(T \to B\), so that it deserves the designation of relative cotangent complex \(L^{X/Y}_{\bullet}\) of \(X\) over \(Y\). One verifies likewise that for every affine open of \(X\) lying over an affine open of \(Y\), it is given (up to canonical isomorphism) by the procedure of 4.3.

In the case where \(f\) is a local complete intersection, one sees moreover that one even obtains a perfect complex, which then allows one to define again an element \(T_f\) in \(K'(X)\) by formula (4.3). Let us point out that here again, it would not have been possible in general to define \(T_f\) as an element of \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\), and nothing allows one to suppose that \(T_f\) is globally isomorphic in \(D(X)\) to a bounded complex with locally free components of finite type. This is therefore an additional justification of the point of view put forward in 4.1 in favor of the "sophisticated" \(K'(X)\), in preference to \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\).

4.5 Subject to the question raised in 4.2, we have therefore again gathered all the structural elements needed to give a meaning to (1.2) for a proper local complete intersection morphism of arbitrary schemes (the noetherian hypothesis made implicitly in the preceding sections being in fact removable without essential difficulty). Unfortunately this does not mean that, even subject to this reservation, the formula in question is established, even in the case where \(Y\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(p > 0\), and \(X\) is a proper smooth algebraic scheme of dimension \(3\), say. See the comments in Exp. XIV n° 2.

5. In the form presented in n° 4, the Riemann-Roch formula (1.2) also has a meaning for a proper local complete intersection morphism of complex analytic spaces (or of rigid-analytic spaces in the sense of Tate). But of course the proof given in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) applies to this case only when one supposes in addition that the morphism \(f\) is projective, and as in the algebraic case, it seems that an essentially new idea is needed to treat the general case. The formula we envisage here requires a formula with values in an "analytic Chow ring" \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) or rather \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb{Q}}\), constructed according to the principle explained in 4.2 (the difficulty encountered there vanishing, moreover, owing to the fact that one is in characteristic zero, as is pointed out in Exp. XIV n° 1).

When \(X\) and \(Y\) are non-singular, it is possible to give another version of the Riemann-Roch formula by proceeding as follows. Let first \(X\) be an arbitrary complex analytic space, \(X^{\mathrm{top}}\) the same space equipped with the sheaf of continuous complex functions. We have a canonical morphism of ringed spaces \[X^{\mathrm{top}} \to X ,\] which defines, thanks to the evident contravariant character of the functor \(K'(X)\) in the variable ringed space \(X\), a ring homomorphism \[K'(X) \to K'(X^{\mathrm{top}}) . \tag{5.1}\] Suppose for simplicity that \(X^{\mathrm{top}}\) is compact; then, as in the case (already pointed out in 3.1) of a scheme admitting an ample invertible module, one sees (Exp.II) that every perfect complex on \(X^{\mathrm{top}}\) is globally isomorphic to a bounded complex which is locally free in each degree, i.e. to a complex of complex vector bundles on \(X^{\mathrm{top}}\), and that consequently \(K'(X^{\mathrm{top}})\) is canonically isomorphic to the group \(K(X^{\mathrm{top}})\) well known to topologists \([1]\), defined in terms of complex vector bundles on the compact space \(X^{\mathrm{top}}\). One can therefore interpret the homomorphism (5.1) as being a homomorphism from the \(K'(X)\) defined in terms of the analytic structure, into the well-known ring \(K(X^{\mathrm{top}})\) of the topologists. If now \(f\colon X \to Y\) is a morphism of compact non-singular analytic spaces, one knows how to associate to it, thanks to Atiyah-Hirzebruch \([1]\), a homomorphism of groups \[f_*^{\mathrm{top}}\colon K'(X^{\mathrm{top}}) \to K'(Y^{\mathrm{top}}) . \tag{5.2}\] Using likewise the homomorphism \(f_*\) of (3.1) defined by formula (3.2) (which here implicitly uses Grauert's finiteness theorem \([4]\) for a proper morphism of analytic spaces) one finds a square of natural homomorphisms, involving only groups of type \(K'\) (to the exclusion of rings of Chow-ring or cohomology type): \[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2em{ K'(X) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{f_*} & K'(X^{\mathrm{top}}) \ar[d]^{f_*^{\mathrm{top}}} \\ K'(Y) \ar[r] & K'(Y^{\mathrm{top}}) } \tag{5.3}\] The Riemann-Roch-type formula to which we were alluding consists in asserting the commutativity of this square. One will note the difference in nature between this assertion (which does not neglect torsion phenomena, and takes place in a group \(K'(Y^{\mathrm{top}})\) of an essentially discrete nature), and the Riemann-Roch formula of the type envisaged in n° 4, taking place in a group of "Chow" type which is no longer of a discrete nature, but which one must on the other hand tensor with \(\mathbb{Q}\). Formula (5.3) is proved in any case when \(X\) and \(Y\) are non-singular projective varieties \([7]\), as is the formula of the type envisaged in n° 4, which is proved in this case in \([\mathrm{RRR}]\). This is a plausible variant of the index theorem of Atiyah-Singer (and of its generalizations due to Shih \([7]\) and Atiyah), which obviously calls for a common generalization. Once the commutativity of (5.3) is proved, one would conclude from it at once, with the help of the "differentiable Riemann-Roch theorem" of Atiyah-Hirzebruch \([1]\), a cohomological variant of the complex analytic Riemann-Roch formula, namely the commutativity of the square \[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=2em{ K'(X) \ar[r]^-{x \to \operatorname{ch}(x)\operatorname{Todd}(T_X)} \ar[d] & H^{2*}(X,\mathbb{Q}) \ar[d] \\ K'(Y) \ar[r]^-{y \to \operatorname{ch}(y)\operatorname{Todd}(T_Y)} & H^{2*}(Y,\mathbb{Q}) } \tag{5.4}\] where the horizontal arrows are deduced from the cohomological Chern classes, deduced from the homomorphism (5.1) and from the ordinary Chern class homomorphism \[K'(X^{\mathrm{top}}) \to H^{2*}(X,\mathbb{Z}) ,\] the first vertical arrow is the same as in (5.3), and the second is the Gysin homomorphism in rational cohomology.

6. In all of this, we have hardly spoken of anything but the contravariant invariant \(K'(X)\), to the exclusion of the covariant invariant \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) whose existence was pointed out in 3.1. If one regards \(K'(X)\) as giving an analogue of the integral cohomology ring of a topological space, one must regard \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) as giving an analogue of integral homology, the Module structure of \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) over \(K'(X)\) then corresponding to the cap-product operation (∗). The fact that when \(X\) is regular, the natural homomorphism \(K'(X) \to K_{\bullet}(X)\) is an isomorphism, must be regarded as the analogue of the Poincaré duality theorem on oriented topological manifolds, asserting that the cap-product with the fundamental homology class defines an isomorphism from cohomology onto homology. These analogies suggest moreover the advisability of also defining, for a finite polyhedron \(X\) say, an invariant \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) of covariant nature, whose relations to integral homology (spectral sequence, Chern homomorphism...) would be analogous to those existing between \(K'(X)\) and integral cohomology; nor does it seem excluded that there could exist, in terms of these groups \(K_{\bullet}(X)\), variants of the differentiable Riemann-Roch theorem, valid for spaces more general than compact differentiable manifolds.

Returning to the case where \(X\) is an arbitrary noetherian scheme, it is appropriate to equip \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) with an increasing filtration, \(\operatorname{Filt}_i K_{\bullet}(X)\) being generated by classes \(c\ell(F)\), with \(F\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\) such that \(\dim \operatorname{supp} F \leq i\). One will prove (Exp. X) the compatibility of this filtration with the module structure and the filtration of \(K'(X)\), i.e. the formula \[\operatorname{Filt}^i K'(X) \cdot \operatorname{Filt}_j K_{\bullet}(X) \subseteq \operatorname{Filt}_{j-i} K_{\bullet}(X) , \tag{6.1}\] which allows one to equip the graded object associated to the filtered module \(K_{\bullet}(X)\), denoted \[\operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X) ,\] with a module structure over \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\). Its elements can moreover be interpreted as classes of algebraic cycles on \(X\), (for an equivalence relation coarser than rational equivalence, when \(X\) is of finite type over a base field \(K\)). If \[f\colon X \to Y\] is a proper morphism of noetherian schemes, then \[f_*\colon K_{\bullet}(X) \to K_{\bullet}(Y) , \tag{6.2}\] is compatible with the filtrations, and defines a homomorphism of graded groups \[f_*\colon \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(Y) , \tag{6.3}\] giving rise to a "projection formula", i.e. which is \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(Y)\)-linear, a formula which is deduced by passage to the graded objects from the analogous projection formula for the homomorphism (6.2) (which is \(K'(Y)\)-linear).

In the point of view proposed in the Seminar, a workable intersection theory, on noetherian schemes \(X\) not necessarily regular, must consist in the combined study of the contravariant invariants \(K'(X), \operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) and of the covariant invariants \(K_{\bullet}(X), \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X)\), which play roles of essentially different natures, and whose properties complement each other. Thus the covariant groups \(K_{\bullet}(X), \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X)\) are better suited to computation for certain non-proper fibrations, thanks to the "exact homotopy sequence" that one can establish for them ( \([\mathrm{RRR}]\) and Exp. IX); for example one proves (Exp. X) canonical isomorphisms such as \[K_{\bullet}(X[t]) \cong K_{\bullet}(X) , \quad \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X[t]) \cong \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X) ,\] which fail in general for \(K'\) when \(X\) is a non-regular scheme. On the other hand, \(K'(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) lend themselves well to computations thanks to their ring structure.

When one considers schemes \(X\) proper over a field, the projection \[f^X\colon X \to (\mathrm{point})\] defines an "Euler-Poincaré characteristic" homomorphism \[\chi_X = f_*^X\colon K_{\bullet}(X) \to K_{\bullet}(\mathrm{point}) = \mathbb{Z} , \tag{6.4}\] inducing on the subgroup \(\operatorname{Gr}_0(X) = \operatorname{Filt}_0 K_{\bullet}(X)\) the "degree of 0-cycles" homomorphism \[\deg_X\colon \operatorname{Gr}_0(X) \to \mathbb{Z} . \tag{6.5}\] The multiplication homomorphism \[\operatorname{Gr}^i(X) \times \operatorname{Gr}_i(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}_0(X) ,\] composed with the degree homomorphism (6.5), then provides a pairing \[\operatorname{Gr}^i(X) \times \operatorname{Gr}_i(X) \to \mathbb{Z} , \tag{6.6}\] which explains why in certain respects \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X)\) play dual roles, just like cohomology and homology. Thus, if \(f\colon X \to Y\) is a morphism of proper algebraic schemes, the two homomorphisms \[f^*\colon \operatorname{Gr}'(Y) \to \operatorname{Gr}'(X) \quad \text{et} \quad f_*\colon \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X) \to \operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(Y)\] are formally transposes of each other, in the sense of the pairings (6.6).

The homomorphisms (6.4) and (6.5) and the pairing (6.6) deduced from them can be regarded as the source of the numerical relations in intersection theory on algebraic schemes proper over a given field \(k\). This point of view will be developed in some detail in Exp. X, essentially independent of Exp. VI,VII,VIII (centered on the proof of the Riemann-Roch theorem) and of Exp. IX. There we shall also give some notions on the behavior of \(K'(X), \operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) under specialization.

7. As in any intersection theory, it is important to make precise the place which the Picard group (or group of divisor classes in the classical terminology) takes in the theory. Subject to a slight restriction, automatically satisfied when \(X\) is for example quasi-compact, one establishes in Exp. X a canonical isomorphism \[c_1\colon \operatorname{Pic}(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Gr}^1(X) , \tag{7.1}\] which shows the exact role of the Picard group in the theory we propose. This role justifies a more detailed study of this Picard group in Exposés XII, XIII, which are logically independent of the text of the Seminar. A study of numerical equivalence and of certain finiteness theorems for the Picard group (announced without proof in \([6]\)) is carried out by Kleiman in Exp. XIII, using techniques of a purely numerical nature (not using the theory of the Picard functor and of its representability), due to himself and to Mumford, distinctly simpler than the initial proofs of Grothendieck. The only result refractory to Kleiman's methods (and of which he is obliged to make use) is \([6, \text{C-07 (i)}]\), whose proof and certain refinements (including certain representability theorems for the Picard functor) occupy Exposé XII. The latter moreover belongs to a spirit and a technique entirely foreign to the rest of the Seminar, being related to \([5]\); this exposé is logically independent of all the others.

Finally, in Exp. XI, of a distinctly more general nature than XII and XIII and closer to the general spirit of the Seminar, one studies, on an arbitrary locally ringed topos, a remarkable functor called the "determinant functor": \[\det{}^*\colon \operatorname{Parf}(X) \to \operatorname{Inv}(X) \tag{7.2}\] from the category of perfect complexes on \(X\), the morphisms being only the isomorphisms in the sense of the derived category \(D(X)\), to the category of invertible Modules on \(X\). This functor is described more or less completely by the requirement of being of a "local nature", and of being given, for a complex \(L_{\bullet}\) which is bounded and with locally free components, by the formula \[\det{}^*(L_{\bullet}) \cong \bigotimes_i \det(L_i)^{(-1)^i} , \tag{7.3}\] where \(\det(L_i)\) denotes the exterior power of maximal degree of the locally free Module \(L_i\). Although this exposé too is logically independent of the rest of the Seminar with the exception of Exposé I, it nevertheless constitutes an important element of the general "yoga" proposed in the Seminar, and developed for the most part in Exposés I to XI.

footnotes.html: (∗) We shall also allow ourselves to drop the word "locally" in this terminology, which is not likely to cause any confusion.

(∗) This analogy is moreover not purely verbal, and can be made precise, after passage to the associated graded objects \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X)\), by an explicit compatibility relation between the module structure over \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\bullet}(X)\) and the "cap" operation.

Chapter I. \(\lambda\)-Rings (Formal preliminaries)
§ 1. Definitions

We call a \(\lambda\)-ring(∗) a commutative ring \(K\) with unit, equipped with a family of maps \[\lambda^i \colon K \to K \tag{1.1}\] (\(i\) an integer \(\geq 0\)) satisfying the following conditions: \[\lambda^0 x = 1, \quad \lambda^1 x = x, \quad \lambda^n(x + y) = \sum_{i=0}^n \lambda^i x \cdot \lambda^{n-i} y . \tag{1.2}\] Setting, for every \(x \in K\) \[\lambda_t(x) = \sum_{n \geq 0} \lambda^n(x) t^n \in K[[t]] ; \tag{1.3}\] the preceding relations then express that \(x \mapsto \lambda_t(x)\) is a homomorphism from the additive group \(K\) into the multiplicative group \(1 + K[[t]]^+\) of formal power series in \(K\), of augmentation \(1\), lifting the canonical homomorphism \(1 + \sum_{i>0} x_i t^i \mapsto x_1\).

Thus, on the ring \(\mathbb{Z}\) there exists a unique \(\lambda\)-structure compatible with its ring structure and such that \[\lambda_t(1) = 1 + t . \tag{1.4}\] We then have \[\lambda_t(n) = (1 + t)^n , \tag{1.5}\] whence \(\lambda^i(n) = \binom{n}{i}\).
The notion of a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings is clear; an augmented \(\lambda\)-ring is a \(\lambda\)-ring equipped with a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism into \(\mathbb{Z}\).

To define the notion of a special \(\lambda\)-ring, the following considerations are useful. Let \(k\) be a commutative ring with unit, let \(K = 1 + k[[t]]^+\) be the group of formal power series with coefficients in \(k\), of augmentation \(1\); we are going to introduce on it a \(\lambda\)-ring law whose additive structure is the multiplication of formal power series. The multiplication of this ring will be denoted \(f \circ g\). It is entirely determined by the condition that the coefficients \(c_n = c_n(f \circ g)\) of \(f \circ g\) be given by universal polynomials with integer coefficients in the coefficients \(a_i = c_i(f)\) and \(b_i = c_i(g)\) of \(f\) and \(g\) respectively: \[c_n = P_n(a_1,\dots,a_n; b_1,\dots,b_n) , \tag{1.6}\] that it be distributive with respect to the "addition" \(fg\), and be given for linear factors by: \[(1 + at) \circ (1 + bt) = 1 + abt . \tag{1.7}\] The \(P_n\) are computed by computations with symmetric functions; \(P_n\) is isobaric of weight \(n\) in the \(a_i\) and isobaric of weight \(n\) in the \(b_i\) (\(a_i\) and \(b_i\) being of weight \(i\)), and moreover symmetric in \(a = (a_i)\) and \(b = (b_i)\). Likewise, the \(\lambda^i(f)\) are determined unambiguously by the condition that the coefficients \(c_n(\lambda^i(f)) = c_n\) of \(\lambda^i(f)\) be computed by means of universal polynomials with integer coefficients in the coefficients \(c_n(f) = a_n\) of \(f\): \[c_n = P_{i,n}(a_1,\dots,a_{in}) , \tag{1.8}\] that the relations (1.2) be satisfied, and that, for \(i > 1\), we have: \[\lambda^i(1 + at) = 1 \tag{1.9}\] (\(1\) is the zero element of the ring \(K = 1 + k[[t]]^+\)). The \(P_{i,n}\) are again computed by computations with symmetric functions, and one finds that \(P_{i,n}\) is an isobaric polynomial of weight \(in\) in the \(a_i\). Equipped with the preceding operations, \(K\) becomes a \(\lambda\)-ring; its zero element is \(1\), its unit element is \(1 + t\). Let us also note the relation: \[(1 + at) \circ f = f(at) . \tag{1.7 bis}\]

Now let \(K\) be an arbitrary \(\lambda\)-ring. One says that \(K\) is special if the additive homomorphism \(\lambda_t\) from \(K\) into \(1 + K[[t]]^+\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism. This therefore means that we have the formulas: \[\lambda_t(1) = 1 + t, \quad \lambda_t(xy) = \lambda_t(x) \circ \lambda_t(y), \quad \lambda_t(\lambda^i(x)) = \lambda^i(\lambda_t(x)) , \tag{1.10}\] or again, explicitly: \[\begin{cases} \lambda^i(1) = 0 & \text{si } i \geq 1 \\ \lambda^n(xy) = P_n(\lambda^1 x,\dots,\lambda^n x; \lambda^1 y,\dots,\lambda^n y) \\ \lambda^n(\lambda^i(x)) = P_{i,n}(\lambda^1 x,\dots,\lambda^n x) , \end{cases} \tag{1.11}\] where the \(P_n\) and the \(P_{i,n}\) are the universal polynomials envisaged above. In particular, the formulas (1.5) follow from this. (One can show that the \(\lambda\)-ring \(1 + k[[t]]^+\) constructed from a commutative ring \(k\) is special; but we shall not need this fact).
§ 2. Examples

1) Let \(G\) be a group and \(k\) a commutative ring; one considers the group \(K(G)\), the quotient of the free group generated by the classes of representations of \(G\) in \(k\)-modules of finite type by the subgroup generated by the elements of the form \((\rho \oplus \rho') - (\rho) - (\rho')\) (we denote by \(\oplus\) the direct sum). The tensor product and the exterior powers define on \(K(G)\) a \(\lambda\)-ring structure. One can also pass to the quotient by the elements of the form \((\rho) - (\rho') - (\rho'')\) where \(\rho\) is a presented extension of \(\rho'\) by \(\rho''\), trivial as an extension of \(k\)-modules. One obtains the group \(K_r(G)\) of reduced classes of representations of \(G\), and one finds that the \(\lambda\)-ring law passes to the quotient.

When \(k\) is a field which is algebraically closed of characteristic \(0\)(∗), one can show that \(K(G)\), and a fortiori \(K_r(G)\), is a special \(\lambda\)-ring; this is no longer the case if the characteristic is \(\neq 0\), but nevertheless, if \(k\) is algebraically closed (∗), \(K_r(G)\) is still a special \(\lambda\)-ring. To prove this, one is reduced to the case where \(G\) is a product \(\mathrm{Gl}(m,k) \times \mathrm{Gl}(n,k)\) and where one considers only the rational representations of \(G\); our assertion follows from the explicit determination of \(K_r(G)\) which will be made below (example 3); finally, when \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), the complete reducibility of the rational representations of \(G\) shows that \(K(G) = K_r(G)\).

Variants of the preceding example: \(G\) is an algebraic group defined over \(k\) and one considers only the rational representations of \(G\) defined over \(k\); one can consider the case of a topological group, or a Lie group, and continuous representations, etc ... (¹)

2) Let \(X\) be an algebraic variety, irreducible or not, defined over the field \(k\). One may consider the quotient group of the free group generated by the classes of vector bundles on \(X\) (defined over \(k\)), by the subgroup generated by the elements of the form \((E \oplus E') - (E) - (E')\), on which the \(\lambda\)-ring operations will be defined by the tensor product and the exterior powers. If \(X\) is complete, this is a free group generated by the indecomposable vector bundles on \(X\) (thanks to Krull-Remak-Schmidt); the knowledge of this ring and of its basis is equivalent to the complete classification of vector bundles on \(X\) together with the knowledge of the elementary tensor operations on these bundles. If \(k\) is algebraically closed of characteristic \(0\), all the other tensor operations on the classes of bundles are then known, with the help of what will follow in 3). One can carry out a more drastic passage to the quotient, by the group generated by the elements \((E) - (E') - (E'')\) where \(E\) is a vector bundle which is an extension of \(E'\) by \(E''\); one then finds a \(\lambda\)-ring, denoted \(K(\xi(X))\), where \(\xi(X)\) denotes the additive category of vector bundles on \(X\), defined over \(k\). Using the results of example 1), one shows that if \(k\) is algebraically closed (∗), the \(\lambda\)-ring \(K(\xi(X))\) is special, and that the same holds for the first \(\lambda\)-ring defined here, if the characteristic of \(k\) is \(0\), but not in the general case.
3) Determination of \(K(G)\) and of \(K_r(G)\) in certain important cases:

We suppose the field \(k\) algebraically closed, the group \(G\) algebraic, affine connected and "globally reductive" (the radical of \(G\) is isomorphic to \((k^*)^s\)), and we consider only rational linear representations. In characteristic \(0\), the complete reducibility of the rational linear representations of \(G\) shows that \(K(G) = K_r(G)\) is the free group generated by the classes of the irreducible rational representations. Now, if \(T\) is a maximal torus of \(G\), we have \(K(T) = K_r(T) = \mathbb{Z}(\hat{T})\), the algebra of the group \(\hat{T}\) of rational characters of \(T\); we have \(\hat{T} \simeq \mathbb{Z}^r\) if \(r\) is the rank of \(G\) (this is independent of the characteristic). The Weyl group \(W\) acts on \(T\), hence on \(K(T)\), in a manner which depends only on the actions of \(W\) on the free group \(\hat{T}\). We have an evident homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings, deduced from the injection of \(T\) into \(G\): \[K_r(G) \to K_r(T)^W = K(T)^W , \tag{1.13}\] where \(K(T)^W\) denotes the set of fixed points of \(W\) in \(K(T)\). The theory of linear representations of \(G\) (∗) then proves the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1

The homomorphism (1.13) is an isomorphism.

We see therefore that \(K_r(G)\), once the "type" of \(G\) is known (characterized by \(W\) acting on a group \(\mathbb{Z}^r\)), does not depend on the characteristic, and that it is a special \(\lambda\)-ring. We have a canonical basis of \(K_r(G)\) corresponding to the orbits of \(W\) in \(\hat{T}\); another basis is obtained as follows: let \((\rho_i)_{1 \leq i \leq r'}\) be the classes of representations of \(G\) corresponding to the fundamental representations of \(G/\operatorname{rad} G\), and \((\sigma_i)_{1 \leq i \leq r''}\) a basis of the group of rational homomorphisms of \(G\) into \(k^*\); we thus have \(r' + r'' = r\) (rank of \(G\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

The ring \(K_r(G)\) is identified with the subring \(\mathbb{Z}[\rho_1,\dots,\rho_{r'}; \sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_{r''}; \sigma_1^{-1},\dots,\sigma_{r''}^{-1}]\) of the field of rational functions in the \(\rho_i\) and the \(\sigma_j\) with coefficients in \(\mathbb{Q}\)(∗).

In particular, \(K_r(G)\) has no divisors of \(0\); if \(G = \prod_i \mathrm{Gl}(n_i,k)\), the ring \(K_r(G)\) can be made explicit as follows: let \(\rho_i\) be the representation of \(G\) deduced from the identity representation of \(\mathrm{Gl}(n_i,k)\); if one then considers the field of rational functions over \(\mathbb{Q}\) in indeterminates \(\lambda^j(\rho_i)\) (\(1 \leq j \leq n_i\)), then \(K_r(G)\) is identified with the subring generated by these indeterminates and the inverses of the \(\lambda^{n_i}(\rho_i)\). This justifies our assertion that, at least in characteristic \(0\), the operations \(\lambda^i\) make it possible to reconstitute all the tensor operations on vector bundles; this is still true over an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic, provided one takes reduced classes.

Remarks: a) In example 1), the ring \(K(G)\) is augmented, the augmentation being defined by the degree of the representations, and by passage to the quotient, this augmentation defines an augmentation on \(K_r(G)\). Likewise, the \(\lambda\)-rings envisaged in example 2) are augmented when \(X\) is \(k\)-irreducible; in \(K(\xi(X))\), the augmentation is defined by the rank of a vector bundle, that is, the dimension of its fibers.
b) When the field \(k\) is not algebraically closed, it is plausible that \(K(G)\) is a special \(\lambda\)-ring in characteristic \(0\), and likewise for \(K_r(G)\) in all characteristics.
c) In non-zero characteristic, Theorem 1.1 was graciously provided to me by Chevalley.
§ 3. The \(\lambda\)-ring defined by a graded ring

Let \(A\) be a commutative graded ring with unit; we shall suppose for simplicity that the degrees of \(A\) are positive and that \(A^0 = \mathbb{Z}\). We denote by \(\hat{A}\) the product of the \(A^i\) (it is a commutative ring with unit), by \(\hat{A}^+\) the kernel of the evident augmentation \(\hat{A} \to A^0 = \mathbb{Z}\). Then \(1 + \hat{A}^+\) is a subgroup of the multiplicative group of invertible elements of \(\hat{A}\). Consider the abelian group \[\tilde{A} = \mathbb{Z} \times (1 + \hat{A}^+) , \tag{1.14}\] whose elements will be denoted \([n,x]\) with \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\) and \(x = 1 + \sum_{i \geq 1} x_i \in 1 + \hat{A}^+\) (\(x_i \in A^i\)). We shall write this group additively, thus: \[[n,x] + [n',x'] = [n + n', xx'] . \tag{1.15}\] The zero element of this group is \([0,1]\). We are going to introduce on it a structure of augmented \(\lambda\)-ring, compatible with its additive structure, the augmentation being \(\varepsilon\colon[n,x] \to n\). The product \([m,x][n,y]\) is completely characterized by the fact that it can be written \([mn, x^n y^m (x \ast y)]\), where \(x \ast y\) is an element of \(1 + \hat{A}^+\) whose components are expressed as universal polynomials with integer coefficients in the \(x^i\) and the \(y^i\): \[(x \ast y)^i = Q_i(x^1,\dots,x^i; y^1,\dots,y^i) \quad (i \geq 1) \tag{1.16}\] the polynomial \(Q_i\) being isobaric of weight \(i\). Moreover, \(x \ast y\) must be distributive with respect to the "addition" \(xy\) and for "linear" factors must reduce to the following: \[[1, 1 + x^1][1, 1 + y^1] = [1, 1 + x^1 y^1] . \tag{1.17}\] The polynomials \(Q_i\) are evaluated by computations with elementary symmetric functions (this is the computation of the Chern classes of a tensor product of vector bundles with the help of the Chern classes \(x^i\) and \(y^i\) of the factors and their respective dimensions \(m\) and \(n\)). From (1.17) one deduces: \[(1 + x^1) \ast (1 + y^1) = (1 + x^1 y^1)/(1 + x^1)(1 + y^1) , \tag{1.17 bis}\] and an arbitrary product \(x \ast y\) is computed term by term by decomposing \(x\) "formally" into a product \(\prod_{i=1}^n (1 + \alpha_i)\) and \(y\) into a product \(\prod_{i=1}^n (1 + \beta_i)\), where the \(\alpha_i\) and the \(\beta_i\) are of degree \(1\).

In this way, \(\tilde{A}\) becomes an augmented commutative ring, with a unit \([1,1]\), and one can interpret it as the ring obtained by adjoining a unit to the ring \(1 + \hat{A}^+\). Moreover, on \(\tilde{A}\), one defines a filtration by setting \(\tilde{A}^0 = \tilde{A}\) and by denoting by \(\tilde{A}^n\) the set of the \([0,x]\) with \(x^i = 0\) for \(1 \leq i < n\); this filtration is compatible with the product, for we have \[\left(1 + \sum_{i \geq m} x^i\right) \ast \left(1 + \sum_{j \geq n} y^j\right) = 1 - \frac{(m+n-1)!}{(m-1)!(n-1)!} x^m y^n + \dots , \tag{1.18}\] the unwritten terms being of degree \(> m + n\). This formula shows that the graded ring \(G(\tilde{A})\) associated to \(\tilde{A}\) is identified with \(A\) from the additive point of view, but not from the multiplicative point of view; one can only say that the map \(\eta'\) from \(A\) into \(G(\tilde{A})\) defined by \[\eta'(x^i) = (-1)^{i-1}(i - 1)! \, x^i \quad (x^i \in A^i) \tag{1.19}\] is a ring homomorphism.

One introduces on \(\tilde{A}\) operations \(\lambda^i\) satisfying the axioms of \(\lambda\)-rings (§ 1, formulas (1.2)), well determined by the condition that the components of \(\lambda^i[0,x]\) be deduced from the components \(x^i\) of \(x\) by universal polynomials with integer coefficients \[\lambda^i[0,x] = [0, \lambda^i x] \qquad (\lambda^i x)^{(n)} = Q_{i,n}(x^1,\dots,x^n) \tag{1.20}\] (\(Q_{i,n}\) is isobaric of weight \(n\)), and that we have \[\lambda^i[1, 1 + x^1] = 0 \quad \text{pour } i > 1 . \tag{1.21}\] The polynomials \(Q_{i,n}\) are evaluated by a computation with elementary symmetric functions, which is the computation of the Chern classes of the exterior powers of a vector bundle, with the help of the Chern classes and the rank of the latter. One then finds that \(\tilde{A}\) is an augmented \(\lambda\)-ring which is special, and that the \(\tilde{A}^i\) are stable under the operations \(\lambda^j\).

The augmented \(\lambda\)-ring \(\tilde{A}\) can be regarded as a functor in \(A\); consequently the principal automorphism \(x = \sum_{i \geq 0} x^i \to \check{x} = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i x^i\) of \(A\) defines an automorphism of \(\tilde{A}\), denoted by the same symbol \(\vee\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

For \(x \in \tilde{A}\) of the form \([n, 1 + x^1 + \dots + x^n]\) (we thus have \(x^i = 0\) for \(i > n\)), we have \(\lambda^i(x) = 0\) for \(i > n\), we have \(\lambda^n(x) = [1, 1 + x^1]\) and finally \[\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \lambda^i(x) = [0, 1 - (n-1)! \, x^n + \dots] . \tag{1.22}\]

From this proposition and from the fact that the \(\tilde{A}^i\) are stable under the operations \(\lambda^j\), one deduces that formula (1.22) is valid whenever \(\varepsilon(x) = n\), whether or not we have \(x^i = 0\) for \(i > n\).

Formula (1.22) admits the following generalizations: let \(K\) be any \(\lambda\)-ring, otherwise arbitrary; for \(x \in K\), we set \[\gamma^n(x) = (-1)^n \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \lambda^i(x + n) = \lambda^n(x + n - 1) , \tag{1.23}\] (the identity of the last two members follows from an immediate induction). We have in particular \(\gamma^0(x) = 1\) and \(\gamma^1(x) = x\); we then set: \[\gamma_t(x) = \sum_{n \geq 0} \gamma^n(x) t^n \in K[[t]] \tag{1.24}\] from which one deduces the formula: \[\gamma_t(x) = \lambda_{t/(1-t)}(x) , \tag{1.25}\] which is equivalent to \[\lambda_s(x) = \gamma_{s/(1+s)}(x) . \tag{1.25 bis}\] This proves that the \(\lambda^i\) are expressed in terms of the \(\gamma^i\) and that \(\gamma_t(x+y) = \gamma_t(x)\gamma_t(y)\), and consequently that the maps \(\gamma^i\) define a new \(\lambda\)-ring structure on \(K\). Formula (1.22) then shows that for \(K = \tilde{A}\) and every \(x \in \tilde{A}\), we have \[\gamma^n(x - \varepsilon(x)) = [0, 1 + (-1)^{n-1}(n-1)! \, x^n + \dots] \tag{1.22 bis}\] and consequently, the component \(x^n\) is obtained, up to the factor \((-1)^{n-1}(n - 1)!\), by reducing modulo \(\tilde{A}^{n+1}\) the element \(\gamma^n(x - \varepsilon(x))\) of \(\tilde{A}^n\).
The Chern homomorphism:

For every graded ring \(A\) satisfying the preceding conditions, we shall define a homomorphism of augmented rings: \[\operatorname{ch} \colon \tilde{A} \to \hat{A} \otimes \mathbb{Q} . \tag{1.26}\] This homomorphism is defined unambiguously by the condition that it be an additive homomorphism, that it transform \(1\) into \(1\), that the components of \(\operatorname{ch}(x)\) in degree \(> 0\) be given by universal isobaric polynomials with rational coefficients in the components of \(x\), and that \[\operatorname{ch}([1, 1 + x^1]) = \exp x^1 = \sum_{n \geq 0} (x^1)^n/n! . \tag{1.27}\] One then obtains at once the explicit expression of \(\operatorname{ch}\): let \(\eta\) be the additive endomorphism of \(\hat{A} \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) defined for \(x^i\) of degree \(i\) by \[\eta(x^i) = \frac{1}{(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!} \, x^i \tag{1.28}\] we then have \[\operatorname{ch}\left(\left[n, 1 + \sum_{i \geq 1} x^i\right]\right) = n + \eta\left(\log\left(1 + \sum_{i \geq 1} x^i\right)\right) . \tag{1.29}\] As one can "invert" this formula, at least if \(A\) has only a finite number of homogeneous components, one sees that in this case, we have an isomorphism \(\operatorname{ch}\) of the ring \(\tilde{A} \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) onto the ring \(\hat{A} \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) (which completely elucidates the structure of \(\tilde{A} \otimes \mathbb{Q}\)).

Recall that according to Hirzebruch [1], a formal power series \(f \in \mathbb{Q}[[t]]\) defines, by universal polynomial formulas, an additive homomorphism \(1 + \hat{A}^+ \to 1 + (\hat{A} \otimes \mathbb{Q})^+\), denoted \(\mathscr{T}_f\); when \(f(t) = t/(1 - \exp(-t))\), one simply writes \(\mathscr{T}_f = \mathscr{T}\) (\(\mathscr{T}\) is the initial of Todd). One extends the definition of \(\mathscr{T}(x)\) to \(x \in \tilde{A}\). This said, we have the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3

Let \(N \in \tilde{A}\) be of the form \([q, 1 + N^1 + \dots + N^q]\) (we have \(N^i = 0\) for \(i > q\)). If we set \[\lambda_{-1}(N) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \lambda^i(N) = \sum_{i=0}^q (-1)^i \lambda^i(N) ,\] we shall have: \[\operatorname{ch}(\lambda_{-1}(N)) = (-1)^q N^q \, \mathscr{T}(\check{N})^{-1} , \tag{1.30}\] which is equivalent to: \[\operatorname{ch}(\lambda_{-1}(\check{N})) = N^q \, \mathscr{T}(N)^{-1} . \tag{1.30 bis}\]
§ 4. The operations \(\lambda^p(N,x)\)

Let \(A\) be the ring of formal power series, with coefficients in \(\mathbb{Z}\), in two infinite sequences of indeterminates, denoted \(\lambda^i N\) and \(\lambda^i x\) (\(i \geq 1\)). We set \(\lambda^1 N = N\) and \(\lambda^1 x = x\), and we introduce on \(A\) the unique special \(\lambda\)-ring structure for which we have \(\lambda^i(N) = \lambda^i N\) and \(\lambda^i(x) = \lambda^i x\) and for which the operations \(\lambda^i\) are "continuous" (if we restricted ourselves to polynomials in indeterminates \(\lambda^i N\) and \(\lambda^i x\), we would have the "free \(\lambda\)-ring generated by \(N\) and \(x\)"; our ring \(A\) is a completion of it). One can then form \[\lambda_{-1}(N) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \lambda^i N , \tag{1.31}\] which is a formal power series with constant term \(1\), hence invertible; one can therefore define unambiguously an element \(\lambda^p(N,x)\) of \(A\) by setting \[\lambda^p(N,x)\lambda_{-1}(N) = \lambda^p(x\lambda_{-1}(N)) . \tag{1.32}\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.4

Let \(J_q\) be the closed ideal of \(A\) generated by the \(\lambda^i N\) with \(i > q\); let us identify \(A/J_q\) with the ring of formal power series in the \(\lambda^i x\) and the \(\lambda^j N\) (\(1 \leq j \leq q\)). Then \(\lambda^p(N,x)\) reduced modulo \(J_q\) is a polynomial, isobaric of weight \(p\) with respect to the variables \(\lambda^i x\).

Let \(N\) and \(F\) be two vector spaces of finite dimensions \(q,q'\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(0\); let \(N^{(p)}\) be the kernel of the map \((a_1,\dots,a_p) \to a_1 + \dots + a_p\) from \(N^p\) into \(N\). We let the symmetric group \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) act on \(N^p\), hence on \(N^{(p)}\), hence on \({\textstyle\bigwedge}(N^{(p)})\), then on \(\bigotimes^p F\) and on \({\textstyle\bigwedge}(N^{(p)}) \otimes \bigotimes^p F\). Let us consider this vector space as a graded module over \(\mathfrak{S}_p \times \mathrm{Gl}(N) \times \mathrm{Gl}(F)\) and take its "alternating part", which is a graded module over \(\mathrm{Gl}(N) \times \mathrm{Gl}(F) = G\). We then have \[\lambda^p(N,F) = \mathrm{E}_G\left(\left({\textstyle\bigwedge}(N^{(p)}) \otimes \bigotimes^p F\right)^{\mathrm{alt}}\right) . \tag{1.33}\] N.B. If \(M\) is a complex of \(G\)-modules, we denote by \(\mathrm{E}_G(M)\) the alternating sum in \(K(G)\) of the classes of the components of \(M\); it is the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(M\); in formula (1.33), the left-hand side \(\lambda^p(N,F)\) denotes the element of the ring \(K(G)\) obtained by substituting for the variables \(\lambda^i N\) and \(\lambda^i F\) the classes in \(K(G)\) of the \(G\)-modules \(\wedge^i(N)\) and \(\wedge^i(F)\) respectively.

Proof: By expanding the second member of (1.32) by the second formula (1.11), one sees that the first assertion is proved if one proves the analogous assertion obtained by setting \(x = 1\), i.e. if one proves that in the \(\lambda\)-ring \(\mathbb{Z}[\lambda^1 N,\dots,\lambda^q N]\) where \(\lambda^i N = 0\) for \(i > q\), the element \(\lambda^p(\lambda_{-1}(N))\) is divisible by \(\lambda_{-1}(N)\). A priori, the quotient \(\lambda^p(N,1) = \lambda^p(\lambda_{-1}(N))/\lambda_{-1}(N)\) is a formal power series contained in the field of quotients \(K\) of the ring of formal power series in the \(\lambda^i N\) for \(1 \leq i \leq q\). On the other hand, let us also denote by \(N\) a vector space of dimension \(q\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(0\), and let us consider the element \(\ell^p(N) = \mathrm{E}_G(({\textstyle\bigwedge}(N^{(p)}))^{\mathrm{alt}}) \in K(G)\), where now we have \(G = \mathrm{Gl}(N)\). One knows (§ 2, Theorem 1.1) that \(K(G)\) is identified with the subring of \(K\) generated by the \(\lambda^i N\) for \(1 \leq i \leq q\) and by the inverse of \(\lambda^q N\). On the other hand, one easily verifies in this ring that we have \(\ell^p(N) \lambda_{-1}(N) = \lambda^p(\lambda_{-1}(N))\). Since we are in a big field \(K\) and since \(\lambda_{-1}(N) \neq 0\), we conclude \(\ell^p(N) = \lambda^p(N,1)\); the two members lie in the intersection of \(K(G)\) and of the ring of formal power series in the \(\lambda^i N\), hence are polynomials in the \(\lambda^i N\).

To prove (1.33) in the general case, one verifies that the second member satisfies in \(K(G)\) the formula analogous to (1.32), that is, that its product by \(\lambda_{-1}(N)\) is \(\lambda^p(F \lambda_{-1}(N))\). Likewise, the product of \(\lambda^p(N,F) \in K(G)\) by \(\lambda_{-1}(N)\) is \(\lambda^p(F \lambda_{-1}(N))\), as follows from the fact that \(K(G)\) is special. As \(K(G)\) is an integral domain (cf § 2) and as \(\lambda_{-1}(N) \neq 0\) (same reference), we have indeed proved formula (1.33). qed.

Remarks: a) When the dimension \(q'\) of \(F\) is \(\geq p\), then (1.33) characterizes the polynomial \(\lambda^p(N,x)\) in the \(\lambda^i N\) (\(1 \leq i \leq q\)) and the \(\lambda^j x\) (\(1 \leq j \leq p\)).

b) It is plausible that (1.33) is still satisfied if the field \(k\) is not algebraically closed(∗). This question would have to be resolved if one wanted to prove the Riemann-Roch theorem over a field of characteristic \(0\) which is not algebraically closed, by the method we are about to expound. The passage from a \(\mathfrak{S}_p\)-module to its alternating part moreover has a reasonable meaning only if the base field is of characteristic \(0\).

Now let \(K\) be an arbitrary special \(\lambda\)-ring and let \(N\) be an element of \(K\) such that \(\lambda^i N = 0\) for \(i\) sufficiently large. One can then, for every \(x \in K\) and every integer \(p \geq 1\), consider the elements \(\lambda^p(N,x)\), which are expressed by universal polynomials with integer coefficients in the \(\lambda^i N\) and the \(\lambda^j x\). We have \(\lambda^1(N,x) = x\) and formula (1.32) holds in \(K\). To express the properties of the operations \(x \mapsto \lambda^p(N,x)\), it is convenient to introduce a new \(\lambda\)-ring denoted \(K_N\). As a commutative ring, \(K_N\) is obtained by adjoining a unit to the ring whose underlying additive group is \(K\) and whose multiplication is given by \[x_N y = xy\mu \qquad (\mu = \lambda_{-1}(N)) . \tag{1.34}\] The map \(\lambda_t\) from \(K_N\) into \(K_N[[t]]\) is defined by: \[\lambda_t(n,x) = (1 + t)^n \sum_{p \geq 0} \lambda^p(N,x) t^p , \tag{1.35}\] where we have set \(\lambda^0(N,x) = (1,0)\) (unit element of \(K_N\)). I claim that, equipped with these operations, \(K_N\) is a special \(\lambda\)-ring. It is thus a matter of verifying the formulas \(\lambda_t(X + Y) = \lambda_t(X) \lambda_t(Y)\), \(\lambda_t(XY) = \lambda_t(X).\lambda_t(Y)\), and \(\lambda_t(\lambda^i(X)) = \lambda^i(\lambda_t(X))\) for \(X,Y \in K_N\). One may restrict oneself to verifying these formulas when \(X\) and \(Y\) are in \(K\), and one finds that it suffices to verify it when \(K\) is the free special \(\lambda\)-ring generated by \(X,Y\) and \(N\) subject to the relations \(\lambda^i N = 0\) for \(i > q\). But the homomorphism of groups \[K_N \to K , \tag{1.36}\] which transforms unit into unit and on \(K\) reduces to the homomorphism \(x \to x\mu\), is a homomorphism compatible with the ring structures and the maps \(\lambda^i\), as one verifies at once. In the present case, its restriction to \(K\) is injective; as \(K\) is a special \(\lambda\)-ring, it follows easily that the above relations are indeed satisfied for \(X,Y \in K\), and this completes the proof of our assertion.

According to formula (1.23), it is natural, for \(x \in K\) and \(n \geq 1\), to set \[\gamma^n(N,x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{i} \lambda^i(N,x) , \tag{1.37}\] in other words, we set \(\gamma^n(N,x) = \gamma^n(x_N)\), where \(x_N\) denotes \(x\) considered as an element of the \(\lambda\)-ring \(K_N\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5

Let \(A\) be a commutative graded ring with unit, \(N\) an element of \(\tilde{A}\) of the form \(N = [q, 1 + N^1 + \dots + N^q]\) and \(x\) an arbitrary element of \(\tilde{A}\): \(x = [\nu, 1 + \sum_{i \geq 1} x^i]\). Then, for every integer \(j \geq 0\), we have \(\gamma^{q+j}(N,x) \in \tilde{A}^j\), and the component of degree \(j\) of \(\gamma^{q+j}(N,x)\) is of the form \[(\gamma^{q+j}(N,x))^{(j)} = (-1)^{j-1}(j - 1)! \, G_{q,j}(\nu,x^1,\dots,x^j; N^1,\dots,N^j) , \tag{1.38}\] where \(G_{q,j}\) is a universal polynomial with integer coefficients characterized by the condition \[G_{q,j}(\nu,x^1,\dots,N^j) \, N^q = (-1)^q (x \ast \lambda_{-1}(N))^{(q+j)} . \tag{1.39}\]

Proof. We may suppose that \(A\) is the ring of polynomials with integer coefficients in indeterminates \(x^i\) and \(N^j\) (with \(1 \leq j \leq q\)). As we evidently have, combining (1.32) and (1.37), \[\gamma^n(N,x)\lambda_{-1}(N) = \gamma^n(x \lambda_{-1}(N)) , \tag{1.40}\] and as, for \(n = q + j\), the second member is of filtration \(\geq q + j\) (use formula (1.22 bis), taking into account that \(x \lambda_{-1}(N)\) has zero augmentation), while \(\lambda_{-1}(N)\) is of filtration \(q\) \(formule (1.22)\), it follows that \(\gamma^n(N,x)\) is of filtration \(j\), since by virtue of (1.18) the graded ring associated to \(A\) is here an integral domain. Using the preceding formulas, one immediately obtains the indicated form for \((\gamma^{q+j}(N,x))^{(j)}\). qed.

footnotes.html: (∗) In this seminar, we rather say pre-\(\lambda\)-ring (V 2.1), reserving the name \(\lambda\)-ring for those satisfying the supplementary condition of page 3 (cf V 2.4).
(∗) It is unnecessary for \(k\) to be algebraically closed, cf.(VI 3.3) for an even more general result (encompassing all those which will be used in the present report).

(¹) One can construct other examples of \(\lambda\)-rings, for example the set of equivalence classes of quadratic forms, the set of classes of representations of a Lie algebra, etc... We promise that we shall not need all these examples to treat the Riemann-Roch theorem!
(∗) An unnecessary condition, cf. the preceding footnote.
(∗) Cf. Séminaire Chevalley 1956/58 Groupes de Lie algébriques (Ecole Normale Supérieure).
(∗) One must suppose the derived group \(G'\) of \(G\) simply connected for this statement to be correct.
(∗) This is indeed proved by the same method.

pages.title: Chapter II. Classes of coherent algebraic sheaves and Chern classes

blocks.html: Chapter II. Classes of coherent algebraic sheaves and Chern classes
§ 1. Chow theory

Throughout this chapter, we suppose fixed a base field \(k\) which we shall suppose algebraically closed, for simplicity. A large part of what follows, and perhaps all of it, is however valid without this restriction. The algebraic spaces and the regular maps considered will be defined over \(k\).
An algebraic space is said to be quasi-projective if it is isomorphic to a locally closed subset of a projective space.

Let \(X\) be a connected quasi-projective non-singular variety of dimension \(n\). One denotes by \(A(X)\) and calls the Chow ring of \(X\) the graded ring of classes of cycles on \(X\) up to rational equivalence. (\(A^i(X)\) being formed by the classes of cycles of dimension \(n - i\)). The fact that \(A(X)\) is indeed a graded ring follows from the following theorem of Chow:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1(∗)

Every cycle on \(X\) is rationally equivalent to a non-singular cycle. Given elements of \(A^i(X)\) and \(A^j(X)\), one can find non-singular representatives of these elements, say \(Y^{n-i}\) and \(Z^{n-j}\), which intersect everywhere transversally.

(A cycle is said to be non-singular if its components are non-singular subvarieties. One says that two cycles intersect everywhere transversally if every component of one intersects every component of the other everywhere transversally, i.e. if the two intersecting varieties are non-singular at the points of intersection and if their tangent varieties there are in general position ...)

Let \(f\) be a morphism (i.e. a regular map) from \(X\) into \(Y\) (\(X\) and \(Y\) being quasi-projective and non-singular); then \(f\) defines (using the preceding theorem) a homomorphism of graded rings: \[f^* \colon A(Y) \to A(X) \tag{2.1}\] (inverse image of cycle classes). Thus \(A(X)\) becomes a contravariant functor in \(X\).

Let \(f\) be a proper morphism from \(X^n\) into \(Y^m\) (i.e., in the terminology of Weil, the graph of \(f\) is complete over \(Y\), cf \([3]\)). Then \(f\) also defines an additive homomorphism \[f_* \colon A(X^n) \to A(Y^m) \tag{2.2}\] preserving the dimension of cycles, hence raising the degree by \(m - n\).

Thus, with respect to proper morphisms, and disregarding the multiplicative structures and the gradings, \(A(X)\) is a covariant functor in \(X\). Let us note the classical formula \[f_*(x.f^*(y)) = f_*(x).y . \tag{2.3}\]

We shall also use the Chow theory of Chern classes. It consists in attaching, to every vector bundle \(E\) on \(X\) (always supposed quasi-projective non-singular), Chern classes \(C^i(E) \in A^i(X)\), (\(i \geq 1\)), so as to satisfy the following conditions; we set \(C^0(E) = 1\) and \[\tilde{C}(E) = \left[\operatorname{rang} E, \sum_{i \geq 0} C^i(E)\right] \in \tilde{A}(X) \tag{2.4}\] (see Chapter I, § 3 for the definition of \(\tilde{A}(X)\)). The conditions to be verified by a theory of Chern classes are then expressed as follows: \[\tilde{C}(E) = \tilde{C}(E') + \tilde{C}(E'') \tag{2.5}\] if the vector bundle \(E\) is an extension of \(E'\) by \(E''\) (if we set \(C(E) = \sum_{i \geq 0} C^i(E)\), this condition is expressed \(C(E) = C(E').C(E'')\)). Formula (2.5) makes it possible to extend \(\tilde{C}\) to an additive homomorphism from \(K(\xi(X))\) (cf. Chap. I, § 2, example 2) into \(\tilde{A}(X)\): \[\tilde{C} \colon K(\xi(X)) \to \tilde{A}(X) , \tag{2.6}\] and one requires that \(\tilde{C}\) be a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings; this last condition can also be written \[\tilde{C}(E \otimes F) = \tilde{C}(E) \tilde{C}(F) \qquad \tilde{C}(\wedge^i E) = \lambda^i \tilde{C}(E) , \tag{2.7}\] for two vector bundles \(E\) and \(F\) (of course, \(\tilde{C}\) is automatically compatible with the augmentations). Let \(D\) be a divisor on \(X\) and \(L(D)\) the vector bundle it defines; one requires \[C^1(L(D)) = \ell(D) \qquad C^i(L(D)) = 0 \quad (i \geq 1) \tag{2.8}\] (where for an arbitrary cycle \(Z\), we denote by \(\ell(Z)\) its class in \(A(X)\)). Finally, one imposes that the \(C^i\) be functorial, i.e. that if \(f\) is a morphism from \(X\) into \(Y\) and \(E\) a vector bundle on \(Y\), we have \[C^i(f^!(E)) = f^*(C^i(E)) , \tag{2.9}\] where \(f^!(E)\) is the inverse image of \(E\) on \(X\). One can also write (2.9) in the following form: the notion of inverse image of a bundle defines a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism of augmented \(\lambda\)-rings(∗) \[f^! \colon K(Y) \to K(X) \tag{2.10}\] (one writes here \(K(X)\) instead of \(K(\xi(X))\)), and (2.9) means that we have commutativity in the diagram: \[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ K(Y) \ar[r]^{f^!} \ar[d]_{\tilde{C}_Y} & K(X) \ar[d]^{\tilde{C}_X} \\ A(Y) \ar[r]_{f^*} & A(X) }\] (the symbol \(\tilde{C}\) has been given as a subscript the space on which it is considered.)

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2

There exists a theory of Chern classes satisfying conditions (2.5), (2.7), (2.8) and (2.9).
We shall also assume the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\) and let \(U\) be its complement; if \(x\) is an element of \(A(X)\) which induces \(0\) on \(U\), there exists a representative of \(x\) which is a cycle carried by \(Y\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

Let \(p = \dim X - \dim Y\). Then, if \(i < p\), the homomorphism \(A^i(X) \to A^i(U)\) is bijective. Every element of the kernel of \(A^p(X) \to A^p(U)\) is a linear combination of components of dimension \(n - p\) of \(Y\) (hence is proportional to \(\ell(Y)\) if \(Y\) is irreducible)
§ 2. Definition of the Chern classes of coherent algebraic sheaves

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category, and let \(\mathcal{C}_o\) be a subclass of \(\mathcal{C}\). One denotes by \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) the quotient group of the free group generated by the classes (up to isomorphism) of elements of \(\mathcal{C}_o\), by the subgroup generated by the elements of the form \((E) - (E') - (E'')\), where \(E\) is an extension of \(E'\) by \(E''\) (\(E,E',E'' \in \mathcal{C}_o\)). One must suppose that the classes of elements of \(\mathcal{C}_o\) for the isomorphism relation form a set. One denotes by \(\gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o}(E)\) the class of a \(E \in \mathcal{C}_o\) in \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\), and one omits the index \(\mathcal{C}_o\) when there is no risk of confusion. The group \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) is the solution of a universal problem relative to the functions \(E \to f(E)\) defined on \(\mathcal{C}_o\), with values in an arbitrary abelian group \(A\), and which are additive in the sense that \(f(E) = f(E') + f(E'')\) whenever \(E\) is an extension of \(E'\) by \(E''\).
