Let \(F\) be an additive functor from \(\mathcal{C}\) into \(\mathcal{C}'\) and \(\mathcal{C}_o'\) a subclass of \(\mathcal{C}'\) satisfying the same condition as \(\mathcal{C}_o\), such that for every \(A \in \mathcal{C}_o\), the \(R^q F(A)\) are in \(\mathcal{C}_o'\) and zero for \(q\) sufficiently large (one supposes that in \(\mathcal{C}\) injective resolutions exist, so that the derived functors \(R^q F\) of \(F\) exist); then \(F\) defines a homomorphism from \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) into \(K(\mathcal{C}_o')\), again denoted \(F\), by the formula \[F(\gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o}(E)) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o'}(R^i F(E)) \tag{2.11}\] More generally, if one has a cohomological functor \((F^i)\) from \(\mathcal{C}\) into \(\mathcal{C}'\) such that for every \(E \in \mathcal{C}_o\) the \(F^i(E)\) are in \(\mathcal{C}_o'\) and zero except for a finite number, one deduces from it a homomorphism from \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) into \(K(\mathcal{C}_o')\). These remarks extend to multifunctors; moreover, the "classical" spectral sequences give transitivity properties which we shall not make explicit.

In particular, if \(F\) is an exact functor, formula (2.11) simplifies: \[F(\gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o}(E)) = \gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o'}(F(E)) . \tag{2.11 bis}\] This applies in particular if \(\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}'\), if \(F\) is the identity functor and if \(\mathcal{C}_o \subset \mathcal{C}_o'\): we have a canonical homomorphism from \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) into \(K(\mathcal{C}_o')\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.3

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and \(\mathcal{C}_o \subset \mathcal{C}_1\) two subclasses such that the classes (up to isomorphism) of objects of \(\mathcal{C}_1\) form a set. We suppose the following conditions satisfied

(i) For every exact sequence \(0 \to A' \to A \to A'' \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{C}\) such that \(A\) and \(A''\) are in \(\mathcal{C}_o\) (resp. \(\mathcal{C}_1\)), we have \(A' \in \mathcal{C}_o\) (resp. \(A' \in \mathcal{C}_1\)).

(ii) Let \(u \colon A \to B\) and \(v \colon C \to B\) with \(A, B, C \in \mathcal{C}_1\) and \(u\) surjective; then there exist \(L \in \mathcal{C}_o\) and homomorphisms \(u' \colon L \to C\) and \(v' \colon L \to A\) such that \(u'\) is surjective and \(vu' = uv'\) .

(iii) For every \(A \in \mathcal{C}_1\), there exists an integer \(d = d(A)\) such that for every left resolution \(L\) of \(A\) by objects of \(\mathcal{C}_o\), we have \(Z_d(L) \in \mathcal{C}_o\) (\(Z_d\) denotes the cycles of dimension \(d\)).

Under these conditions, the canonical homomorphism from \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) into \(K(\mathcal{C}_1)\) is an isomorphism.

Principle of the proof: let \(A \in \mathcal{C}_1\); by virtue of (ii) and (iii) there exists a finite resolution \(L\) of \(A\) by objects of \(\mathcal{C}_o\); let us then set \(f(A) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o}(L^i)\). One shows that the element of \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) thus constructed does not depend on the chosen resolution \(L\): if we have a second resolution \(L'\), we "cap" \(L\) and \(L'\) by a third resolution \(L''\), with surjective homomorphisms from \(L''\) into \(L\) and \(L'\) (which is possible thanks to (i) and (ii); we are then reduced to proving \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathcal{C}_o}(L) = \mathrm{E}_{\mathcal{C}_o}(L'')\). Now the difference of the two is \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathcal{C}_o}(N)\) where \(N\) is the kernel of the surjective homomorphism from \(L''\) onto \(L\), a kernel which is in \(\mathcal{C}_o\) by (i). Now \(N\) is a complex which is acyclic in all dimensions, whence \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathcal{C}_o}(N) = 0\), whence the conclusion (N.B. \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathcal{C}_o}(L)\), for a finite complex with values in \(\mathcal{C}_o\), denotes the Euler-Poincaré characteristic \(\sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma_{\mathcal{C}_o}(L^i)\) of \(L\)).

One proves in an analogous way that the function \(f \colon \mathcal{C}_1 \to K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) thus constructed is additive, hence defines a homomorphism from \(K(\mathcal{C}_1)\) into \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\), and it is immediate that this homomorphism is inverse to the canonical homomorphism from \(K(\mathcal{C}_o)\) into \(K(\mathcal{C}_1)\). qed.
Remark: To verify (ii), it suffices to verify that \(\mathcal{C}_1\) is contained in a subclass \(\mathcal{C}_2\) of \(\mathcal{C}\) satisfying the two conditions
(ii a) Every \(A \in \mathcal{C}_2\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a \(L \in \mathcal{C}_o\) .
(ii b) If \(u \colon A \to B\), with \(A,B \in \mathcal{C}_2\), then \(\operatorname{Ker} u \in \mathcal{C}_2\) .

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

Let \(X\) be a quasi-projective algebraic space. Let \(\mathscr{F}(X)\) be the category of coherent algebraic sheaves on \(X\), \(\xi(X)\) the category of locally free coherent algebraic sheaves (equivalent to the category of vector bundles on \(X\)), \(\mathscr{F}_o(X)\) the category of coherent algebraic sheaves \(F\) such that for every \(x \in X\), \(F_x\) is a \(\mathcal{O}_x\)-module of finite cohomological dimension (we thus have \(\xi(X) \subset \mathscr{F}_o(X) \subset \mathscr{F}(X)\)). Then the canonical homomorphism \[K(\xi(X)) \to K(\mathscr{F}_o(X)) \tag{2.12}\] is an isomorphism.

We shall verify the conditions of Theorem 2.3. Conditions (i) and (iii) follow from the definition of cohomological dimension and from the fact that a projective module of finite type over the noetherian local ring \(\mathcal{O}_x\) is free. To verify (ii), we shall prove that \(\mathcal{C} = \mathscr{F}(X)\) satisfies conditions (ii a) and (ii b) of the remark. The second is trivial; it thus remains to prove the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4 (Serre)

Let \(X\) be a quasi-projective algebraic space. Then every coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\) is isomorphic to the quotient of a locally free algebraic sheaf.

This proposition is true if \(X\) is a closed subset of projective space by virtue of \([2]\), since, for \(n\) sufficiently large, \(F(n)\) is generated by its sections, which means that \(F\) is a quotient of a \(\mathcal{O}(-n)^k\). In the general case, \(X\) is an open subset of a closed subset of a projective space, and it suffices to use the following result (due to Cartier and Serre in particular cases):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5

Let \(F\) be a coherent algebraic sheaf on an open subset \(U\) of an algebraic space \(X\). Then \(F\) is isomorphic to the restriction of a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\).

By Zornifying over the open sets containing \(U\) to which one can extend \(F\), we are reduced to the case where \(X\) is affine. Let \(\bar{F}\) be the algebraic sheaf on \(X\) whose sections over an open set \(V\) are the sections of \(F\) over \(U \cap V\). One easily finds that \(\bar{F}\) is quasi-coherent (i.e. defined on every affine open \(V\) — hence here on all of \(X\) — by a module, of finite type or not, over the coordinate ring of the open set in question). This is immediate if \(U\) is itself affine, for \(\bar{F}\) is then defined on the open set \(V\) by \(\Gamma(U \cap V,F)\) considered as a module over \(\Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\). In the general case, \(U\) is a finite union of affine open sets \(U_i\), hence \(\bar{F}\) is the kernel of an evident homomorphism of quasi-coherent sheaves: \[\sum_i \bar{F}\,|\,U_i \to \sum_{i,j} \bar{F}\,|\,U_i \cap U_j\] hence is itself quasi-coherent. As \(X\) is supposed affine, \(\bar{F}\) is therefore generated by its sections. As \(\bar{F}\,|\,U = F\) is coherent, there exists a finite number of sections of \(\bar{F}\) which generate \(\bar{F}\,|\,U\). These sections generate a coherent subsheaf of \(\bar{F}\), whose restriction to \(U\) is isomorphic to \(F\). qed.

Suppose now \(X\) quasi-projective non-singular. Then, by virtue of the syzygy theorem, we have, with the notations of the corollary to Theorem 2.3, \(\mathscr{F}_o(X) = \mathscr{F}(X)\). In this case, we therefore have: \[K(\xi(X)) = K(\mathscr{F}(X)) . \tag{2.13}\] This isomorphism can be expressed as follows:

blocks.title: Corollary 2 to Theorem 2.3
blocks.html:
Corollary 2 to Theorem 2.3

Let \(X\) be a non-singular quasi-projective variety. For every abelian group \(A\), there is a one-to-one correspondence between the additive functions \(f\) of vector bundles on \(X\), with values in \(A\), and the additive functions \(g\) of coherent algebraic sheaves on \(X\), with values in \(A\). To every \(f\) corresponds the unique \(g\) such that we have: \[f(E) = g(\mathcal{O}_X(E)) \tag{2.14}\] for every vector bundle \(E\) on \(X\), denoting by \(\mathcal{O}_X(E)\) the sheaf of germs of regular sections of \(E\) over \(X\).

To compute \(g(F)\) for an arbitrary coherent algebraic sheaf, one considers a finite resolution of \(F\) by sheaves of the form \(\mathcal{O}_X(E_i)\), and one then has: \[g(F) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i f(E_i) . \tag{2.14 bis}\] In particular, if \(X\) is connected, we envisaged in § 1 an additive function \(\tilde{C}(E)\) of the vector bundle \(E\), with values in the group \(\tilde{A} = \tilde{A}(X)\). From it one deduces an additive function of coherent sheaves, say \(\tilde{C}(F)\), and by passing to the components, functions \(F \to C^i(F) \in A^i(X)\). The \(C^i(F)\) are called the Chern classes of the coherent sheaf \(F\); they can theoretically be computed from the Chern classes of vector bundles by (2.14 bis) (but where one must pass, in the second member, to the multiplicative notation). As for the component of \(\tilde{C}(F)\) along \(\mathbb{Z}\), it is the rank of the sheaf \(F\), which corresponds to the rank of a module over an integral domain: if \(X\) is affine (a case to which one can always reduce), \(F\) is defined by a module of finite type over the coordinate ring of \(X\), and one takes the rank of this module ....
§ 3. Functorial generalities on \(K(X)\)

If \(X\) is an algebraic space, we set for brevity \(K(X) = K(\mathscr{F}(X))\), and when \(X\) is quasi-projective non-singular, we identify this group with \(K(\xi(X))\) by virtue of Corollary 2 to Theorem 2.3. As \(K(\xi(X))\) is not only an abelian group, but a \(\lambda\)-ring, it follows by transport of structure that \(K(X)\) is also a \(\lambda\)-ring, hence is equipped with a multiplicative structure and with maps \(\lambda^i\). It is important to define these structures directly, without passing to vector bundles, which will be done below.

If \(E\) is a vector bundle on \(X\), we denote by \(\gamma(E)\) its class in \(K(X)\); if \(F\) is a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\), \(\gamma(F)\) likewise denotes its class in \(K(X)\). If \(Y\) is an irreducible subvariety of \(X\), we set \(\gamma(Y) = \gamma(\mathcal{O}_Y)\), where \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) denotes the sheaf of local rings of \(Y\), considered as a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\). One defines by linearity the symbol \(\gamma(Y)\) when \(Y\) is an arbitrary cycle on \(X\). When there are risks of confusion, one writes \(\gamma_X\) for \(\gamma\).

Let \(X\) be an arbitrary algebraic space. One introduces on \(K(X)\) a filtration, by denoting by \(K(X)^i\) the subgroup of \(K(X)\) generated by the \(\gamma(F)\) with \(\dim.\operatorname{supp} F \leq n - i\) (where \(n\) is the dimension of \(X\)). From Serre's "dévissage lemma", in the form given in \([3]\), follows the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.6

The group \(K(X)^i\) is generated by the \(\gamma(Y)\), where \(Y\) runs over the set of subvarieties of dimension \(\leq n - i\) of \(X\). If \(X\) is irreducible, we have \(G^o(K(X)) = \mathbb{Z}\) .
This last isomorphism is given by the homomorphism from \(K(X)\) into \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined with the help of the notion of rank of a coherent algebraic sheaf.

Suppose now \(X\) non-singular. Taking inspiration from the developments at the beginning of § 2, one defines a multiplication on \(K(X)\) by setting \[\gamma(F) \gamma(G) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma(\operatorname{Tor}_i(F,G)) \tag{2.15}\] (the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i\) being taken, of course, with respect to the local rings of \(X\)). The exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i\) shows that formula (2.15) indeed defines a biadditive map from \(K(X) \times K(X)\) into \(K(X)\); associativity follows from the spectral sequence of the multiple Tor. One also verifies in this way that one can compute the product of several factors by the formula: \[\gamma(F_1)\dots\gamma(F_p) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma(\operatorname{Tor}_i(F_1,\dots,F_p)) \tag{2.16}\] where, in the second member, the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i\) are "simultaneous Tor", computed by simultaneous projective resolution of the arguments \(F_i\).

If one supposes in (2.15) that \(G\) is locally free, one finds: \[\gamma(F) \gamma(G) = \gamma(F \otimes G) , \tag{2.15 bis}\] which shows in particular that \(\gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the unit element of \(K(X)\), and that the canonical homomorphism from \(K(\xi(X))\) into \(K(X)\) is a ring homomorphism.

Formula (2.15) is evidently suggested by Serre's "Tor formula" in intersection theory. This formula proves the second part of the following proposition, the first resulting immediately from formula (2.15) and a computation of local \(\operatorname{Tor}_i\):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7

Let \(X\) be a non-singular variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\) two cycles in \(X\). If \(Y\) and \(Z\) intersect everywhere transversally, we have: \[\gamma(Y) \gamma(Z) = \gamma(Y.Z) \tag{2.17}\] If \(Y\) and \(Z\) are homogeneous and of respective dimensions \(n - i\) and \(n - j\), and if they intersect without excess component, we then have: \[\gamma(Y) \gamma(Z) = \gamma(Y.Z) \mod K(X)^{i+j+1} . \tag{2.18}\]

One should beware that one does not in general have equality in (2.18). We shall see in § 4 that, if \(X\) is quasi-projective non-singular, the ring structure of \(K(X)\) is compatible with its filtration and that the associated graded ring \(G(K(X))\) is identified with a quotient of \(A(X)\) by a torsion subgroup (perhaps always reduced to \(0\)(∗)).

I know how to define the \(\lambda^i(F)\) directly in terms of sheaves only if the field \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\) (this is the reason which has prevented us from proving the Riemann-Roch theorem when \(k\) is not of characteristic \(\neq 0\)). Let \(F\) be a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\) on which the group \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) of permutations of \(p\) letters acts; one then denotes by \((F)^{\mathrm{alt}}\) the set of the \(x \in F\) such that \(s.x = \varepsilon(s)x\) for every \(s \in \mathfrak{S}_p\) (\(\varepsilon(s)\) is the signature of \(s\)). It is evidently a coherent algebraic subsheaf of \(F\). This said, we have:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.8

Let \(X\) be a quasi-projective non-singular variety over the field \(k\) of characteristic \(0\). Let \(F\) be a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\); we then have \[\lambda^p(\gamma(F)) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma((\operatorname{Tor}_i(F,F,\dots,F))^{\mathrm{alt}}) \tag{2.19}\]
N.B. It is clear that the group \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) acts on the sheaf \(\operatorname{Tor}_i(F,F,\dots,F)\).
Principle of the proof: one considers a finite resolution \(L\) of \(F\) by locally free sheaves \(L_i\), and one uses the following general lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9

Let \(L = (L_i)\) be a graded locally free coherent algebraic sheaf, having a finite number of non-zero components. We have in \(K(X)\) the formula: \[\mathrm{E}\left(\left(\textstyle\bigotimes^p L\right)^{\mathrm{alt}}\right) = \lambda^p(\mathrm{E}(L)) \tag{2.20}\]

In this formula, we consider \(\bigotimes^p L\) as a sheaf on which the group \(\mathfrak{S}_p\) acts taking the gradings into account (which therefore introduce signs in the well-known way, cf Cartan-Eilenberg); moreover, if \(M\) is a graded coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\), we set \(\mathrm{E}(M) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \gamma(M_i)\).

To prove formula (2.20), we are reduced to proving the analogous formula in \(K(G)\), where \(G\) is an algebraic group (a product of groups \(\mathrm{Gl}(n,k)\) if one insists) and \(L\) a \(G\)-module of finite dimension over \(k\) (cf. Chap. I, § 2, example 2, for the definition of \(K(G)\)). In this case, this formula has already been used in Theorem 1.4, to show that the right-hand side of (1.33) satisfies the same functional equation as the left-hand side; the verification of this is elementary. qed.

Let \(f\) be a morphism from \(X\) to \(Y\), where \(Y\) is an algebraic space. The notion of inverse image of a vector bundle defines a homomorphism \[f^! \colon K(\xi(Y)) \to K(\xi(X))\] of \(\lambda\)-rings. Identifying vector bundles with locally free algebraic sheaves, the notion of inverse image of a vector bundle corresponds to the notion of inverse image of an algebraic sheaf (already used in [3, n° 8]). In the case where \(Y\) is nonsingular, one defines more generally a homomorphism of groups: \[f^! \colon K(Y) \to K(X) \tag{2.21}\] by a formula involving an alternating sum of Tor, inspired by the developments at the beginning of § 2, and which the reader will make explicit. We then have commutativity in the following diagram: \[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ K(\xi(Y)) \ar[r]^{f^!} \ar[d] & K(\xi(X)) \ar[d] \\ K(Y) \ar[r]_{f^!} & K(X) }\] which shows in particular, when \(X\) and \(Y\) are both nonsingular and quasi-projective, that the two definitions of \(f^!\) coincide under the identification of \(K(X)\) with \(K(\xi(X))\) and of \(K(Y)\) with \(K(\xi(Y))\).

Another case in which one can define a homomorphism (2.21), without \(Y\) necessarily being nonsingular, is the one in which the functor \(F \to f^!(F)\) from \(\mathscr{F}(Y)\) to \(\mathscr{F}(X)\) is exact, i.e. when the local rings of \(X\) are flat modules over the local rings of \(Y\). It then suffices to set \(f^!(\gamma_Y(F)) = \gamma_X(f^!(F))\). This case occurs in particular when \(X\) is a locally trivial fiber space over \(Y\) (to see this, one is reduced to the case of a product \(X = T \times Y\)). Let us note that one also verifies that in this case, we have \(f^!(\mathcal{O}_Z) = \mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(Z)}\) if \(Z\) is an irreducible subvariety of \(Y\), whence: \[f^!(\gamma_Y(Z)) = \gamma_X(f^{-1}(Z)) \tag{2.22}\] if \(X\) is "flat" over \(Y\). Without this last condition, formula (2.22) becomes incorrect in general, but we have the following analogue of Proposition 2.7:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.8

Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a morphism of nonsingular varieties, and let \(Z\) be a cycle on \(Y\). If \(Z\) is everywhere transversal to \(f\), formula (2.22) is valid; if \(Z\) is homogeneous of codimension \(i\) and if \(f^{-1}(Z)\) has no excess component, we have: \[f^!(\gamma_Y(Z)) \equiv \gamma_X(f^{-1}(Z)) \mod K(X)^{i+1} . \tag{2.23}\]
Proof analogous to that of Proposition 2.7.
Let us finally point out that we shall see in § 4 that, when \(X\) and \(Y\) are quasi-projective and nonsingular, then \(f^!\) is compatible with the filtrations of \(K(X)\) and of \(K(Y)\).

Let again \(X\) and \(Y\) be two arbitrary algebraic spaces, and \(f\) a proper morphism from \(X\) to \(Y\). For every coherent algebraic sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), the direct image sheaf \(f_!(F)\) is coherent algebraic, and the same holds more generally for the sheaves \(R^q f_!(F)\) (cf. [3]). Recall that, by definition, \(R^q f_!(F)\) is the sheaf on \(Y\) associated to the presheaf which to the open set \(U\) assigns the group \(H^q(f^{-1}(U),F)\); we have in particular: \[R^0 f_!(F) = f_!(F) , \quad R^q f_!(F) = 0 \quad (q > \dim X) , \tag{2.24}\] moreover: \[\Gamma(U,R^q f_!(F)) = H^q(f^{-1}(U),F) \tag{2.25}\] if the open set \(U\) is affine. Following § 2, one defines a homomorphism from \(K(X)\) to \(K(Y)\), again denoted \(f_!\), by the formula \[f_!(\gamma_X(F)) = \sum_{q \geq 0} (-1)^q \gamma_Y(R^q f_!(F)) . \tag{2.26}\] Thus \(K(X)\) becomes a covariant functor in \(X\), with respect to proper morphisms. The relation \((fg)_! = f_! g_!\) (just like the relation \((fg)^! = g^! f^!\) which we forgot to point out in its proper place) is a particular case of the transitivity relations to which we alluded in § 2, and which follow from the spectral sequences of composite functors.

Let us note the following formula, valid for \(Y\) nonsingular (and particularly easy to verify when \(Y\) is moreover quasi-projective, by taking for \(y\) the class of a vector bundle on \(Y\)): \[f_!(x.f^!(y)) = f_!(x).y \qquad (x \in K(X) , y \in K(Y)) . \tag{2.27}\] In the particular case where the inverse images of the points of \(Y\) under \(f\) are finite, and contained in an affine open set(∗), one observes that the functor \(F \to f_!(F)\) is exact and that \(R^q f_!(F) = 0\) for \(q > 0\); in this case, we therefore have \(f_!(\gamma_X(F)) = \gamma_Y(f_!(F))\). In particular, if \(X\) is an algebraic subspace of \(Y\) and if \(i\) is the canonical injection of \(X\) into \(Y\), we have: \[i_!(\gamma_X(F)) = \gamma_Y(F) , \tag{2.28}\] where, in the right-hand side, \(F\) is considered as a sheaf on \(Y\) which is zero outside \(X\).
§ 4. Some technical results
Let \(X\) be an algebraic space; we saw in § 3 that the projection \(p\) of \(X \times k\) onto \(X\) defines a homomorphism \[p^! \colon K(X) \to K(X \times k) . \tag{2.29}\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.9

The homomorphism (2.29) is surjective.

We argue by induction on \(n = \dim X\); the assertion is trivial if \(n < 0\); suppose \(n \geq 0\); by virtue of Proposition 2.6, it suffices to verify that for every irreducible closed subset \(Y\) of \(X \times k\), \(\gamma(Y)\) is in the image of \(p^!\). It suffices to continue the argument assuming \(X\) irreducible; if \(p(Y)\) is not dense in \(X\), our assertion follows from the induction hypothesis; otherwise, either we have \(Y = X \times k\) and the assertion is trivial, or else \(Y\) is a hypersurface in \(X \times k\). Let then \(U\) be an affine open subset of \(X\) consisting of nonsingular points; if \(D\) is the divisor on \(U \times k\) induced by \(Y\), we know (commutative algebra) that it is linearly equivalent to a divisor of the form \(D' \times k\), where \(D'\) is a divisor on \(U\). Then \(\gamma_{U \times k}(D) - \gamma_{U \times k}(D' \times k)\) belongs to \(K(U \times k)^2\) (corollary of Proposition 2.10), and by what we have already proved, this element is of the form \(p^!(x')\) with \(x' \in K(U)\). Hence \(\gamma_{U \times k}(D) = p^!(y')\) with \(y' = x' + \gamma_U(D')\); but \(y'\) is the restriction of an element \(y\) of \(K(X)\) (Prop. 2.10), and consequently the restriction of \(\gamma_{X \times k}(D) - p^!(y)\) to \(U \times k\) is zero; by Proposition 2.10, \(\gamma_{X \times k}(D) - p^!(y)\) comes from an element of \(K(X \times k - U \times k) = K(X' \times k)\) with \(X' = X - U\). The proof is completed by applying the induction hypothesis once more. qed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

Let \(X\) be an algebraic space and \(p\) the projection of \(X \times k^n\) onto \(X\); then the homomorphism \(p^! \colon K(X) \to K(X \times k^n)\) is surjective; it is even bijective if \(X\) is nonsingular.
Induction on \(n\) for the first assertion. For the second, we set \(f(x) = (x,0)\), whence \(pf = 1\) and \(f^! p^! = 1\), which proves that \(p^!\) is injective.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.10

Let \(X\) be an algebraic space, \(U\) an open subset of \(X\) and \(Y\) its complement in \(X\). Then the sequence of natural homomorphisms: \[K(Y) \to K(X) \to K(U) \to 0 \tag{2.30}\] is exact.
N.B. Compare with Proposition 2.3.

The fact that \(K(X) \to K(U)\) is surjective follows at once from Proposition 2.5 (or also from Proposition 2.6). It remains to prove that if \(x \in K(X)\) has zero restriction to \(K(U)\), it is in the image of \(K(Y)\). For this, we return to the explicit definition of \(K(X)\) and of \(K(U)\): we have two coherent algebraic sheaves \(F\) and \(G\) on \(X\) with \(x = \gamma_X(F) - \gamma_X(G)\) and \(\gamma_U(F) - \gamma_U(G) = 0\); this last relation means that we can find coherent sheaves \(P'\) and \(Q'\) on \(U\), equipped with filtrations whose factors are pairwise isomorphic on \(U\), and such that we have an isomorphism on \(U\): \[P = F + Q' \to Q = G + P' .\] By virtue of Proposition 2.5, the sheaves \(P'\) and \(Q'\) are restrictions to \(U\) of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (denoted by the same letters); likewise, their filtrations come from filtrations on the corresponding sheaves on \(X\), by virtue of Lemma 2.11 below. We then have: \[\gamma_X(F) - \gamma_X(G) = (\gamma_X(P) - \gamma_X(Q)) + (\gamma_X(P') - \gamma_X(Q')) .\] Introducing the factors \(P'_i\) and \(Q'_j\) of the filtrations of \(P'\) and \(Q'\) on \(X\), the last term is the sum of terms \(\gamma_X(P'_i) - \gamma_X(Q'_j)\), where \(P'_i|U \simeq Q'_j|U\). We are thus reduced to proving the following particular case: if \(R\) and \(S\) are coherent sheaves on \(X\) such that \(R|U \simeq S|U\), then \(\gamma_X(R) - \gamma_X(S)\) is in the image of \(K(Y)\). Now let \(T\) be the graph of the given isomorphism from \(R|U\) to \(S|U\); it is a coherent subsheaf of \((R \times S)|U\), hence by virtue of Lemma 2.11, it is the restriction to \(U\) of a coherent subsheaf, again denoted \(T\), of \(R \times S\). We then have \(\gamma_X(R) - \gamma_X(S) = (\gamma_X(R) - \gamma_X(T)) - (\gamma_X(S) - \gamma_X(T))\) and it suffices to prove for example that the first term of the right-hand side is in the image of \(K(Y)\). Now the projection \(u \colon T \to R\) is an isomorphism on \(U\), hence \(\gamma_X(R) - \gamma_X(T) = \gamma_X(\operatorname{Coker} u) - \gamma_X(\operatorname{Ker} u)\) and \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) and \(\operatorname{Coker} u\) have their support in \(Y\).
It thus remains to prove the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.11

Let \(X\) be an algebraic space, \(F\) a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(X\), \(U\) an open subset of \(X\) and \(G\) a coherent subsheaf of \(F|U\). Then \(G\) is the restriction to \(U\) of a coherent subsheaf of \(F\).

Let \(\bar{G}\) be the subsheaf of \(F\) whose sections over the open subset \(V\) of \(X\) are the sections of \(F\) over \(V\) whose restriction to \(U \cap V\) is a section of \(G\). Everything comes down to proving that this sheaf \(\bar{G}\) is coherent. For this, we may assume \(X\) and \(U\) affine (for \(U\) is a union of affine open sets \(U_i\), and \(\bar{G}\) is then the intersection of the analogous sheaves \(\overline{G|U_i}\)). Then the coordinate ring of \(U\) is a ring of fractions \(A_S\) of the coordinate ring \(A\) of \(X\), and one verifies at once that if \(F\) is defined by the \(A\)-module \(M\), then \(F|U\) is defined by the \(A_S\)-module \(M_S = A_S \otimes_A M\). The subsheaf \(G\) of \(F|U\) is then defined by a submodule \(N'\) of \(M_S\), and one sees that \(\bar{G}\) is precisely defined by the submodule \(N\) of \(M\), the inverse image of \(N'\) under the canonical homomorphism from \(M\) to \(M_S\). qed.

blocks.title: Corollary 1 to Proposition 2.10
blocks.html:
Corollary 1 to Proposition 2.10

Let \(x \in K(X)\). For \(x\) to be of filtration \(\leq i\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\), of dimension \(\leq n - i\), such that the restriction of \(x\) to \(X - Y\) is zero.

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Suppose \(X\) is connected of dimension \(n\), without singularities in dimension \(n - 1\). If \(D\) and \(D'\) are two linearly equivalent divisors on \(X\), we have \(\gamma(D) \equiv \gamma(D') \mod K(X)^2\).

By virtue of Corollary 1, we may assume \(X\) nonsingular; the corollary then follows from the general formula: \[\gamma(D) = 1 - \gamma(L(-D)) \mod K(X)^2 , \tag{2.31}\] where \(L(-D)\) denotes the vector bundle of rank 1 defined by the divisor \(-D\). To verify (2.31), we write it in the form: \[\gamma(L(-D)) = 1 - \gamma(D) \mod K(X)^2 , \tag{2.31 bis}\] and we note that the two sides are homomorphisms from the group of divisors into the multiplicative group of invertible elements of the ring \(K(X)/K(X)^2\) (note that \(K(X)^1/K(X)^2\) has square zero, which is verified very easily from the explicit formula (2.15), using Corollary 1). We are thus reduced to the case where \(D\) is an irreducible hypersurface. More generally, if \(D\) is a hypersurface whose components are disjoint, we have the equality: \[\gamma(D) = 1 - \gamma(L(-D)) \tag{2.32}\] as follows from the exact sequence of sheaves \[0 \to \mathcal{O}_X(L(-D)) \to \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_D \to 0 .\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.12

Let \(X\) be a nonsingular quasi-projective variety, and \(Z\) and \(Z'\) two homogeneous cycles of codimension \(p\) on \(X\) which are linearly equivalent. We then have: \[\gamma_X(Z) \equiv \gamma_X(Z') \mod K(X)^{p+1} . \tag{2.33}\]

By hypothesis, we can find a cycle \(Y\) of dimension \(n - p + 1\) on \(X \times k\) (\(n\) being the dimension of \(X\)), and two points \(a,a' \in k\) such that we have: \[Z = f_a^{-1}(Y) , \qquad Z' = f_{a'}^{-1}(Y) ,\] where we set \(f_u(x) = (x,u)\). By virtue of formula (2.23), we then have: \[\gamma_X(Z) \equiv f_a^!(\gamma_{X \times k}(Y)) \mod K(X)^{p+1}\] and an analogous formula for \(\gamma_X(Z')\). It then suffices to prove that \(f_a^! = f_{a'}^!\), which follows at once from Proposition 2.9. qed.

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

The filtration of \(K(X)\) is compatible with its ring structure, i.e. we have \(K(X)^i K(X)^j \subset K(X)^{i+j}\) whatever the integers \(i,j \geq 0\). We have moreover a natural surjective homomorphism of graded rings: \[\varphi \colon A(X) \to G(K(X)) \tag{2.34}\] (where the right-hand side denotes the graded ring associated to \(K(X)\) equipped with the filtration \((K(X)^i)_{i \geq 0}\)) defined by the condition: \[\varphi(Z^{n-p}) = \gamma(Z^{n-p}) \mod K(X)^{p+1} .\]
This corollary follows immediately from Theorem 2.12, Propositions 2.6 and 2.7 and the Chow Theorem 2.1, by induction on the integer \(i + j\).

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two nonsingular quasi-projective varieties and \(f\) a morphism from \(X\) to \(Y\). Then the homomorphism \(f^!\) from \(K(Y)\) to \(K(X)\) is compatible with the filtrations, and we have a commutative diagram: \[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ G(K(Y)) \ar[r]^{G(f^!)} & G(K(X)) \\ A(Y) \ar[r]_{f^*} \ar[u]^{\varphi_Y} & A(X) \ar[u]_{\varphi_X} }\]

Using the graph of \(f\), we decompose \(f\) into the product of an immersion of \(X\) into \(X \times Y\) and a projection of \(X \times Y\) onto \(Y\). For the latter the corollary is trivial, and for an immersion, we proceed as for Corollary 1, using Proposition 2.8 in place of Proposition 2.7.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.13 ("cellular decomposition")

Let \(P\) be an algebraic space (irreducible or not), equipped with an increasing family \(P^0 \subset P^1 \subset \dots \subset P^n = P\) of closed subsets, such that for every \(i\) with \(0 \leq i \leq n\), the connected components of \(P^i - P^{i-1}\) are algebraic spaces \(V_{i,\alpha}\) isomorphic to \(k^i\) (we set \(P^{-1} = \emptyset\)). Then:

a) For every algebraic space \(X\), the natural map from \(K(X) \otimes K(P)\) to \(K(X \times P)\) deduced from the tensor product of sheaves on \(X\) and \(P\), is surjective.

b) The \(\gamma_P(V_{i,\alpha})\) form a system of generators of the group \(K(P)\).

The proof proceeds by induction on \(n\), using the corollary of Proposition 2.9 and Proposition 2.10. (N.B. Unfortunately, I do not know whether the \(\gamma_P(V_{i,\alpha})\) form a basis of \(K(P)\) and whether the homomorphism envisaged in a) is bijective. We shall not need to know this in what follows.)

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.14

Let \(x_i\) (\(1 \leq i \leq q\)) and \(E_j\) (\(1 \leq j \leq r\)) be letters, and \(n_j\) (\(1 \leq j \leq r\)) integers \(> 0\); let us regard the symbols \(\lambda^k(x_i)\) with \(k \geq 1\) and \(\lambda^k(E_j)\) with \(1 \leq k \leq n_j\) as indeterminates and let \(R\) be a polynomial with integer coefficients in these indeterminates. We suppose that, for every graded ring \(A\), when one substitutes in \(R\) for the \(x_i\) elements \(\xi_i\) and for the \(E_j\) elements \(\eta_j\) of \(\tilde{A}\) subject to the restrictions \(\varepsilon(\xi_i) = 0\) and \(\eta_j = [n_j, 1 + \eta_j^{(1)} + \dots + \eta_j^{(n_j)}]\) (hence \(\eta_j^{(k)} = 0\) for \(k > n_j\)), we have \[R((\lambda^k(\xi_i)),(\lambda^k(\eta_j))) \in \tilde{A}^p\] Let then \(X\) be an irreducible nonsingular quasi-projective variety. Whatever the elements \(x_i'\) of \(K(X)\) and the vector bundles \(E_j'\) of dimension \(n_j\) on \(X\) (\(1 \leq i \leq q\), \(1 \leq j \leq r\)), we have \[R((\lambda^k(x_i')),(\lambda^k(\gamma(E_j')))) \in K(X)^p\](∗).

Proof: By virtue of § 2, the \(x_i'\) are themselves differences of elements of the form \(\gamma(E)\), where \(E\) is a vector bundle. On the other hand, assuming \(X\) locally closed in a projective space \(P\), it follows from § 2 and from Proposition 2.5 that, for every \(F \in \mathscr{F}(X)\), the sheaf \(F(n)\) is generated by its sections for \(n\) sufficiently large. If \(F\) is defined by a vector bundle \(E\), this proves that \(E(n)\) is the inverse image of the canonical vector bundle on a Grassmannian variety \(G\) under a morphism from \(X\) to \(G\). Considering the corresponding morphism from \(X\) to \(P \times G\), we see that \(E\) is the inverse image of a vector bundle on \(P \times G\). We therefore see, applying this to all the vector bundles involved, and using Corollary 2 of Theorem 2.12, that we are reduced to the case where \(X\) is a product of Grassmannians. In this case, the conclusion will follow from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.15

Suppose that \(X\) is a product of Grassmannians. Then one can find a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(K(X)\) into a torsion-free \(\lambda\)-ring of the form \(\tilde{A}\) (where \(A\) is a graded ring), compatible with the filtrations, and such that the corresponding homomorphism \(G(K(X)) \to G(A)\) is injective.

To prove the lemma, we shall use the Chow theory of Chern classes; if one could prove the lemma, or Theorem 2.14, directly, one would deduce from it a purely "sheaf-theoretic" theory of Chern classes (cf. § 5).

We shall consider the homomorphism \(\tilde{C}\) from \(K(X)\) to \(\tilde{A}(X)\) and we shall set \(A = A(X)\); we must prove that the homomorphism \(\psi = G(\tilde{C})\) from \(G(K(X))\) to \(G(A(X)) \simeq A(X)\) is injective. The fact that \(\tilde{C}\) is compatible with the filtrations is true for every nonsingular quasi-projective variety and follows easily from the corollary to Proposition 2.3. On the other hand, since \(X\) is a product of Grassmannians \(C_j\), we have on \(X\) vector bundles \(E_j\), inverse images of the canonical bundles on the factors. We shall use the following (known) facts:
a) On \(X\), linear equivalence of cycles is equivalent to numerical equivalence, and \(A(X)\) is a group of finite rank;
b) The \(C^i(E_j)\) generate the ring \(A(X)\).
From a) it follows that \(\varphi \colon A(X) \to G(K(X))\) is injective (hence bijective) by virtue of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.15

Let \(X^n\) be a nonsingular projective variety and \(Z^{n-p}\) a cycle on \(X\) such that \(\varphi(Z) \in G^p(K(X))\) is zero; then \(Z^{n-p}\) is numerically equivalent to zero.

To prove this lemma, it suffices to use the additive function \(\chi(F) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \dim H^i(X,F)\) and the fact that this function is equal to \(1\) for \(F = \mathcal{O}_{(x)}\) for every \(x \in X\).

Thus, \(A(X)\) and \(G(K(X))\) have, in the case under consideration, the same finite rank in every degree, and \(G(K(X))\) is torsion-free, since \(A(X)\) is. From assertion b) above it follows, in a "formal" way, that the map \(\tilde{C}\) from \(K(X)\) to \(\tilde{A}(X)\) is surjective, modulo finite groups. It follows easily, since \(G(K(X))\) and \(A(X)\) have the same finite rank in every degree, that the homomorphism \(G(\tilde{C})\) from \(G(K(X))\) to \(G(\tilde{A}(X))\) is bijective modulo finite groups in every degree (one uses an induction on the degree). Since \(G(K(X))\) is torsion-free, this homomorphism \(G(\tilde{C})\) is therefore injective. qed.
N.B. The homomorphism \(\tilde{C}\) is not surjective in general, even if \(X\) is a projective space of dimension 2.
§ 5. Sheaf-theoretic definition of Chern classes. Application to the study of injection morphisms

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.16

Let \(X\) be a nonsingular quasi-projective variety. Then, for every \(x \in K(X)\), and for every integer \(i \geq 0\), the element \(\gamma^i(x - \varepsilon(x))\) is of filtration \(\geq i\). Let \(c^i(x) \in G^i(K(X))\) be the class of this element modulo \(K(X)^{i+1}\); if we set: \[\tilde{c}(x) = \left[\varepsilon(x), 1 + \sum_{i \geq 1} c^i(x)\right] \in G(K(X)) ,\] then \(\tilde{c}\) satisfies the conditions analogous to those of Theorem 2.2.

The first assertion follows from Theorem 2.14, as does the fact that \(x \to \tilde{c}(x)\) is a homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings (formula (1.25) implies \(\gamma_t(x + y) = \gamma_t(x) \gamma_t(y)\), which already shows that \(\tilde{c}\) is an additive homomorphism). The "functoriality" of \(\tilde{c}\) is verified immediately, and (2.8) follows from (2.31). qed

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

We have \(c^i(x) = \varphi(C^i(x))\).

This follows from a uniqueness theorem for Chern classes, which is proved as in Hirzebruch by passing to flag varieties. One only has to show that if \(Y\) is a flag variety associated to a vector bundle on \(X\), then \(G(K(X)) \to G(K(Y))\) is injective, which is verified by the explicit determination of \(K(Y)\) with all its structures starting from \(K(X)\) (I shall not give the details here).

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Suppose that for every element \(Z \in A(X)\), there exists a morphism \(f\) from \(X\) into a product \(X'\) of Grassmannians such that \(Z\) is in the image of \(f^*\). Then the canonical homomorphisms \[\varphi \colon A(X) \to G(K(X)) , \qquad \psi \colon G(K(X)) \to A(X) ,\] satisfy in dimension \(i\) the following relations: \[\psi \varphi = (-1)^{i-1}(i-1)! \, 1 , \qquad \varphi \psi = (-1)^{i-1}(i-1)! \, 1 . \tag{2.35}\]

Since \(\varphi\) is surjective, it obviously suffices to prove the first formula, and for this one, we may assume that \(X\) is a product of Grassmannians. Since then \(\varphi\) is injective, it suffices to prove the second formula (2.35), which by virtue of Corollary 1 can be written \[\gamma^i(x - \varepsilon(x)) \equiv (-1)^{i-1}(i-1)! \, x \mod K(X)^{i+1}\] for every \(x \in K(X)^i\). Since \(\psi\) is injective, it suffices to prove the analogous relation for \(\tilde{C}(x) \in A(X)^i\), and it is then a consequence of (1.22 bis).

Remark: Corollary 2 makes the validity of (2.35) plausible in all cases; a direct proof of it ought to exist. In any case one easily proves the second formula (2.35) modulo the torsion subgroup of \(G(K(X))\), and in characteristic \(0\), this same formula is a consequence of the Riemann-Roch theorem. The interest of (2.35) (when it is true) lies in the fact that it shows that \(\varphi\) is injective modulo torsion groups (perhaps always zero!(∗)), so that one "does not lose much" in defining the Chern classes as elements of \(G(K(X))\); if one takes the tensor product with \(\mathbb{Q}\), as will be done further on for the Riemann-Roch theorem, the two definitions of Chern classes would then be identical.

Let \(X\) still be quasi-projective nonsingular; let moreover \(Y\) be a nonsingular subvariety of \(X\) and let \(i\) be the injection of \(Y\) into \(X\). We then have, for \(i^!\) and \(i_!\), the following formulas:
(i) \(i^!\) is a homomorphism of \(\lambda\)-rings
(ii) \(i_!(x.i^!(y)) = i_!(x).y\), whence in particular \(i_!i^!(y) = \xi y\) with \(\xi = i_!i^!(1) = \gamma_X(Y)\),
(iii) \(i^!(i_!(x)) = x.\lambda_{-1}(N)\), whence in particular \(i^!(\xi) = i^!i_!(1) = \lambda_{-1}(N)\),

where, by definition, \(N\) is the class in \(K(Y)\) of the conormal bundle to \(Y\) in \(X\). Formulas (i) and (ii) are true for arbitrary morphisms, not necessarily injections, and (iii) and (iv) are verified from the definition (2.15) and the analogous formula for inverse images (by taking for \(x\) and \(x'\) classes of vector bundles, and using the fact that \[\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = \mathcal{O}_Y(\wedge^i N) . ) \tag{2.36}\]

Formula (iv) expresses that \(i_!\) is a ring homomorphism if one introduces on \(K(Y)\) a new multiplicative structure, by the definition \(x_N x' = xx' \lambda_{-1}(N)\) (cf. Chapter I, § 4). It is completed by the formula:
\(v\) \(i_!(\lambda^p(N,x)) = \lambda^p(i_!(x))\) for \(p \geq 1\),

which means that \(i_!\) defines a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(K(Y)_N\) to \(K(X)\). This formula \(v\), certainly true in all cases, has for the moment been proved only in characteristic \(0\), for it follows from the conjunction of Theorems 1.4 and 2.8.
We have analogous formulas for \(i_*\) and \(i^*\):
(i bis) \(i^*\) is a homomorphism of graded rings.
(ii bis) \(i_*(x.i^*(y)) = i_*(x).y\), whence in particular \(i_*(i^*(y)) = \xi' y\) with \(\xi' = i_*(1)\),
(iii bis) \(i^*(i_*(x)) = x.(-1)^q N^q\), whence in particular \(i^*(\xi') = (-1)^q N^q\),
(v bis) \(i_*\) raises degrees by \(q\) units.

In these formulas, \(q\) is the codimension of \(Y\) in \(X\), and we set \(N^i = C^i(N)\). Only formulas (iii bis) and (iv bis) require a proof; I do not know whether they are well known (nor even whether they are true!!!) and I confine myself to noting that they follow by reduction from formulas (ii) and (iv), provided one places oneself in \(G(K(Y))\) and \(G(K(X))\) and not in \(A(Y)\) and \(A(X)\). One then uses the fact that \(i_*\) and \(i^*\) come by passage to the quotient from \(i_!\) and \(i^!\), noting that \(i_!\) shifts the filtration by \(q\) units.
N.B. All the "universal" formulas linking \(i_!\) and \(i^!\) (resp. \(i_*\) and \(i^*\)) are formal consequences of the preceding ones, as one may convince oneself without great difficulty.

From the formula \(v\) follows the formula \[i_!(\gamma^p(N,x)) = \gamma^p(i_!(x)) .\] The conjunction of Theorem 2.14 and Proposition 1.5 shows that \(\gamma^p(N,x)\) is, for \(p = q + j\), of filtration \(\geq j\); if we denote by \(G_p(N,x)\) its class in \(G^j(K(Y))\), Theorem 2.16 shows that we have \[i_*(G_p(N,x)) = c^p(i_!(x)) .\] Finally, Proposition 1.5 together with Theorem 2.14 makes it possible to express \(G_p(N,x)\) as a polynomial with integer coefficients in the \(c^i(N)\), the \(c^i(x)\) and \(\varepsilon(x)\); from this we deduce the theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.17

Suppose the field \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\) and let \(i \colon Y \to X\) be an injection morphism of nonsingular quasi-projective varieties. We then have formulas (i) to \(v\), and moreover, for every integer \(j \geq 0\), the formula: \[c^{q+j}(i_!(x)) = i_*\!\left(\frac{\tilde{C}(x) \ast \lambda_{-1}(\tilde{C}(N))}{(-1)^q N^q}\right)^{(j)} . \tag{2.37}\]
The right-hand side of formula (2.37) is an abbreviated way of denoting the polynomial in the components of \(\tilde{c}(x)\) and \(\tilde{C}(N)\) described in Proposition 1.5.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

Under the preceding conditions, we have: \[\operatorname{ch}(\tilde{c}(i_!(x))) = i_*(\operatorname{ch}(\tilde{c}(x)) \, \mathscr{T}(\check{N})^{-1}) \tag{2.38}\] where \(\operatorname{ch}\) is the Chern homomorphism defined by formula (1.26).

It suffices to use (2.37) written in the form: \[\tilde{c}(i_!(x)) = 1 + i_*\!\left(\frac{\tilde{C}(x) \ast \lambda_{-1}(c(N)) - 1}{(-1)^q N^q}\right) , \tag{2.37 bis}\] the explicit formula (1.29), formula (iv bis) and finally (1.30).

One can write (2.38) in the form \[\operatorname{ch}_X(F) = i_*(\operatorname{ch}_Y(F) \, \mathscr{T}(T_{X/Y})^{-1}) , \tag{2.38 bis}\] where \(F\) is a coherent algebraic sheaf on \(Y\) (considered in the left-hand side as a sheaf on \(X\) which is zero outside \(Y\)), \(\operatorname{ch}_X\) and \(\operatorname{ch}_Y\) are the Chern homomorphisms taken respectively on \(X\) and \(Y\), and \(T_{X/Y}\) is the normal bundle to \(Y\) in \(X\).

Remarks: Contrary to (2.38), formula (2.37) is a "formula without denominators". It has been proved (even in characteristic \(0\)) only by placing oneself in \(G(K(X))\) and not in \(A(X)\), which is perhaps not the same thing. It is certainly true in every characteristic and in \(A(X)\). Let us note that one verifies at once that the images of the two sides under \(i^*\) are the same, whence it follows, applying \(i_*\), that the product of their difference by \(N^q\) is zero. This suggests a completely different method of proof, by trying to obtain \(Y\) as the transversal inverse image of a subvariety \(Y'\) in a variety \(X'\), such that in dimension \(\leq n = \dim X\), the class of \(Y'\) in \(A(X')\) is not a divisor of \(0\).... Unfortunately one cannot hope that \(X'\) is a Grassmannian or anything close to it.
Note that, if one neglects torsion elements, formula (2.38) is equivalent to (2.37).
§ 6. The Riemann-Roch theorem

Its statement is the following: let \(f\) be a proper morphism of a nonsingular quasi-projective variety \(Y\) into a variety \(X\) of the same kind. Then, we have: \[f_*(\operatorname{ch}_Y(x) \, \mathscr{T}(Y)) = \operatorname{ch}_X(f_!(x)) \, \mathscr{T}(X) . \tag{2.39}\] (The two members are in \(A(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) and \(\mathscr{T}(X) = \mathscr{T}(C(T_X))\).)

It allows one to compute, up to torsion elements, the Chern classes of \(f_!(x)\) knowing the Chern classes of \(x\), those of \(T_X\) and \(T_Y\) and the homomorphism \(f_*\). Or again, it allows one to determine the homomorphism \(f_!\) from \(K(Y) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) into \(K(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\), when one identifies these groups, by means of the homomorphisms \(\operatorname{ch}_Y\) and \(\operatorname{ch}_X\), respectively with \(A(Y) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) and \(A(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) (if formula (2.35) is true!), with the aid of \(f_*\) and of the Chern classes of \(T_X\) and of \(T_Y\).

I can prove formula (2.39) only in characteristic \(0\)(∗), and as an equality in \(G(K(X)) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\). For this, \(Y\) being locally closed in a projective space \(P\), one decomposes \(f\) into the product of an injection morphism of \(Y\) into \(P \times X\), and a projection morphism of \(P \times X\) onto \(X\), and one proves (2.39) for each of these two morphisms.

For an injection morphism, one sees immediately that formulas (2.38) and (2.39) are equivalent (the characteristic \(0\) hypothesis intervened only through the intermediary of Theorem 2.8, which allows one to express the \(\lambda^i(\gamma(F))\), for a coherent algebraic sheaf \(F\), by local cohomological invariants of \(F\)).

For a projection morphism \(f \colon P \times X \to X\), one may, thanks to Proposition 2.13, assume that \(x\) is of the form \(y \otimes z\), with \(y \in K(P)\) and \(z \in K(X)\). Then the Künneth formula gives \(f_!(x) = \chi(y)z\), where for a coherent algebraic sheaf \(F\) on \(P\), one sets: \[\chi(F) = \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \dim H^i(P,F) .\] One is then at once reduced to proving the formula \[\chi(F) = \varkappa_r(\operatorname{ch}_P(F) \, \mathscr{T}(P)) \tag{2.40}\] where \(\varkappa_r\) denotes the component of degree \(r = \dim P\). By virtue of Proposition 2.13, one may assume that \(F = \mathcal{O}_Q\), where \(Q\) is a linear subvariety of \(P\). Then \(\chi(F) = 1\), since this is the arithmetic genus of \(Q\); it remains to see that the second member of formula (2.40) equals \(1\).

If indeed \(V\) is a hyperplane of \(P\) and if one sets \(\xi = \gamma_P(\mathcal{O}_V)\) and \(L = \ell(V)\), we shall have: \[1 - \xi = \gamma_P(E(-L))\] (where \(E(-L)\) is the vector bundle defined by \(-L\)), whence: \[C_P(1 - \xi) = [1, 1 - L] ,\] and consequently \[\operatorname{ch}_P(1 - \xi) = e^{-L} , \quad \text{d'où} \quad \operatorname{ch}_P(\xi) = 1 - e^{-L} ,\] and therefore \[\operatorname{ch}_P(\xi^p) = (1 - e^{-L})^p .\] Now if \(p\) is the dimension of \(Q\) in \(P\), we have \(\xi^p = \gamma_P(Q)\); one deduces from this \[\operatorname{ch}_P(F) \, \mathscr{T}(P) = (L/(1 - e^{-L}))^{r+1}(1 - e^{-L})^p\] and it must be verified that the coefficient of \(L^r\) in this formal series in \(L\) equals \(1\), which follows from the characteristic property of the formal series \(L/(1 - e^{-L})\), namely that the coefficient of \(L^q\) in \((L/(1 - e^{-L}))^{q+1}\) equals \(1\) (set \(q = r + 1 - p\)).

Séminaire Chevalley 1958, Anneau de Chow et applications.

(∗) No; for a counterexample cf. this Seminar XIV 4.7.

pages.title: Proof of the Riemann-Roch theorem (Grothendieck, November 1, 1957)

blocks.html: Proof of the Riemann-Roch theorem (Grothendieck, November 1, 1957)
The passages between asterisks ∗ ... ∗ are interpolations by the copyist.
∗ Reminder of notations
[These are those of the "report on Riemann-Roch", cited as R.R.R. in what follows.]

All the varieties considered are nonsingular and quasi-projective (i.e. isomorphic to a locally closed subvariety of a projective space). The base field is algebraically closed, of arbitrary characteristic.

If \(X\) is such a variety, one denotes by \(K(X)\) the abelian group generated by the classes of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (modulo the identification of an extension with a sum); the alternating sum of the Tor makes \(K(X)\) into a ring. If \(F\) is a sheaf (resp. a bundle \(E\) with vector fibre, resp. a subvariety \(Y\) of \(X\)), one denotes by \(\gamma_X(F)\) (resp. \(\gamma_X(E)\), resp. \(\gamma_X(Y)\)) the element of \(K(X)\) corresponding to \(F\) (resp. to \(E\), resp. to \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)). (In fact, one often writes \(F\), \(E\) instead of \(\gamma_X(F)\) ...).
One denotes by \(A(X)\) the graded ring of cycle classes of \(X\) (for rational equivalence - cf. Chow); \(A^i(X)\) is formed of the cycles of codimension \(i\).

If \(E\) is a bundle with vector fibre, one denotes by \(C(E) = \sum C^i(E)\), \(C^i(E) \in A^i(X)\), its Chern class (defined as the inverse image of the Schubert cycles); one deduces from these by linearity the \(C^i(y)\), for every \(y \in K(X)\). If \(C(y) = \prod(1 + a_i)\), one sets, following Hirzebruch: \[\operatorname{ch}(y) = \sum e^{a_i} , \qquad T(y) = \prod \frac{a_i}{1 - e^{-a_i}} .\] These are elements of \(A(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\).
When \(y\) is the tangent bundle to \(X\), one writes \(T(X)\) instead of \(T(y)\).
If \(G\) is a bundle with vector fibre, one denotes by \(\lambda^p G\) its \(p\)-th exterior power, and one sets \(\lambda_{-1}(G) = \sum (-1)^p \lambda^p(G)\); this is an element of \(K(X)\).

If \(f\) is a proper regular map of a variety \(Y\) into a variety \(X\), one defines (additive) homomorphisms: \[f^* \colon A(X) \to A(Y) \quad \text{et} \quad f_* \colon A(Y) \to A(X) .\] The first is homogeneous of degree \(0\) and multiplicative. The second is homogeneous of degree \(\dim.X - \dim.Y\).

One also defines homomorphisms: \[f^! \colon K(X) \to K(Y) \quad \text{et} \quad f_! \colon K(Y) \to K(X) .\] If \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X\), \(f^!(F)\) is equal, by definition, to the alternating sum of the \(\operatorname{Tor}_p^{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_Y, F)\). For bundles with vector fibre, it is simply the inverse-image operation.

If \(G\) is a coherent sheaf on \(Y\), \(f_*(G)\) is coherent on \(X\). If one denotes by \(R^p f_*\) the \(p\)-th derived functor of the functor \(f\) thus defined, we have, by definition, \(f_!(G) = \sum (-1)^p R^p f_*(G)\). ∗

blocks.title: Riemann-Roch Theorem
blocks.html:
Riemann-Roch Theorem

Let \(f \colon Y \to X\) be a proper morphism (∗ regular map ∗) of a nonsingular quasi-projective variety \(Y\) into another one \(X\), and let \(y \in K(Y)\). We have: \[f_*(\operatorname{ch}(y)T(Y)) = \operatorname{ch}(f_!(y))T(X) \quad \text{dans } A(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q} . \tag{1}\]
Proof (∗ abridged ∗).

blocks.title: Lemma 1
blocks.html:
Lemma 1

Consider proper morphisms of varieties \(Z \xrightarrow{g} Y \xrightarrow{f} X\). If theorem R.R. is true for \(f\) and \(g\), it is true for \(f \circ g\). If it is true for \(fg\) and \(g\), it is true for \(f\). If \(f_*\) is injective, and if theorem R.R. is true for \(fg\) and \(f\), it is true for \(g\).
Trivial. (∗ In fact, when one assumes R.R. true for \(fg\) and \(g\), one must further assume that \(g_*\) is surjective in order to be able to assert that R.R. is true for \(f\). ∗)

A proper morphism being the composite of an injection and a projection \(X \times P \to X\) (where \(P\) is a projective space), one is reduced to treating these two cases separately. The second, as we have seen, reduces to standard computation, by means of Proposition 2.13 ("cellular decomposition") of R.R.R. We shall therefore assume \(Y \subset X\), with the injection morphism \(i\). Formula (1) then becomes: \[\operatorname{ch}(i_!(y)) = i_*(\operatorname{ch}(y) T(E)^{-1}) , \tag{2}\] where \(E = T_{X/Y}\) is the normal bundle to \(Y\) in \(X\).

blocks.title: Lemma 2
blocks.html:
Lemma 2

Suppose that there exists on \(X\) a bundle \(G\) with vector fibre of rank \(p = \operatorname{codim}_X Y\), such that: \[\gamma_X(Y) = i_!(1) = \lambda_{-1}(G) ; \quad i^!(G) = \check{E} ; \quad (-1)^p C^p(G) = Y . \tag{3}\] If then we have: \[y = i^!(x) , \quad \text{avec } x \in K(X) , \tag{4}\] formula (2) is valid.
Immediate proof, from the fact that \(\operatorname{ch}\) is a ring homomorphism, and that we have: \[\operatorname{ch}(\lambda_{-1}(G)) = (-1)^p C^p(G) T(\check{G})^{-1} . \tag{5}\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

R.R. is true if \(Y\) is a hypersurface and if \(y\) is of the form \(i^!(x)\), with \(x \in K(X)\).

blocks.title: Lemma 3
blocks.html:
Lemma 3

Suppose that \(X = Y \times P\), where \(P\) is a projective space, and that \(i\) is the injection \(u \to (u,v_0)\) \(v_0 \in P\). Then \(i_*\) is injective and R.R. is true.
This is pure standard computation.

Suppose again \(X\), \(Y\), \(y\) arbitrary (still with \(Y \subset X\)). Let \(X'\) be the variety obtained by blowing up \(X\) by means of \(Y\), and \(Y'\) the hypersurface inverse image of \(Y\). We have a diagram of canonical maps (\(j\) being the canonical map): \[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ Y \ar[r]^{i} & X \\ Y' \ar[r]_{j} \ar[u]^{g} & X' \ar[u]_{f} }\] The inverse image of \(E\) in \(Y'\) admits a sub-bundle of rank 1, whose dual is denoted \(L\). Moreover \(L^{-1}\) is also the restriction to \(Y'\) of the bundle \(L(Y')\) defined on \(X'\) by the divisor \(Y'\). One sets \(F = E/L^{-1}\).
Elementary computations give the following:

blocks.title: Lemma 4
blocks.html:
Lemma 4

We have the formulas (where \(p = \operatorname{codim}_X Y\)): \[f^! i_!(y) = j_!(\lambda_{-1}(\check{F}) y) . \tag{6}\] \[\lambda_{-1}(\check{F}) \equiv \gamma^{p-1}(E) \lambda^p(\check{E}) \mod (1-L) . \tag{7}\] \[f_*(1) = 1 , \quad \text{d'où } f_* f^* = 1 . \tag{8}\] \[f^* i_*(\alpha) \equiv j_*(\alpha C^{p-1}(F)) \mod \operatorname{Ker} f_* \quad \text{pour tout } \alpha \in A(Y) . \tag{9}\]

[To simplify the writing, we have made \(K(Y)\) act on \(K(Y')\) and \(A(Y)\) on \(A(Y')\) by means of the homomorphisms \(g^!\) and \(g^*\). ∗ In formula (7), it seems that \(\gamma^{p-1}(E)\) denotes \(\lambda^{p-1}(E - 2)\), that is to say \(\sum_{i+j=p-1} \binom{-p}{j} \lambda^i(E)\)(∗); the congruence takes place in \(K(Y')\). ∗]
Formula (6) follows from the equality: \[\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal{O}_X(v)}(\mathcal{O}_Y(v), \mathcal{O}_{Y'}(v')) = \wedge^i F(v') \quad (v' \in Y' , v = g(v')) . \tag{6 bis}\]

Formula (7) follows from the formulas \[g_!(L^{-i}) = 0 \quad \text{pour } 0 < i \leq p-1 , \quad g_!(1) = 1 , \tag{10}\] themselves consequences of Künneth and of the cohomology of projective spaces.

Formula (8) is trivial; finally (9) follows from \(g_*(C^{p-1}(F)) = 1\), which is deduced, for example, from the immediate formulas: \[g_*(\xi^i) = 0 \quad \text{si } 0 \leq i < p-1 , \quad g_*(\xi^{p-1}) = 1 , \tag{10 bis}\] with \(\xi = C^1(L) \in A^1(Y')\).
[In fact, (9) is very probably an identity, and not merely a congruence.]
From formula (7) it follows at once that \(y \lambda_{-1}(\check{F}) \in j^!(K(X'))\) provided that we have: \[y \gamma^{p-1}(E) \lambda^p(\check{E}) \in i^!(K(X)) . \tag{11}\]

Suppose this condition (11) satisfied. By the corollary to Lemma 2, R.R. is true for \((Y',X', y \lambda_{-1}(\check{F}))\). On the other hand, to prove (2), it suffices, by virtue of formula (8), to prove that \[f^* \operatorname{ch}(i_!(y)) \equiv f^*(i_*(\operatorname{ch}(y) T(E)^{-1})) \mod \operatorname{Ker} f_* . \tag{12}\] Now, this last formula is verified at once, using (6), (9), R.R. for \((Y',X', y \lambda_{-1}(\check{F}))\) and the formula: \[\operatorname{ch}(\lambda_{-1}(\check{F})) = C^{p-1}(F) T(F)^{-1} \quad \text{(cf. formule (5))} . \tag{13}\]

Thus, R.R. is proved whenever \(y \gamma^{p-1}(E) = 0\), in particular (since \(\gamma^{p-1}(E)\) is of filtration \(\geq p-1\) ∗ in \(K(Y)\) filtered by the codimension of the supports ∗) whenever \(p-1 > \dim Y\), i.e. whenever \(\dim Y < \frac{\dim X - 1}{2}\). Now, one reduces to this case by combining Lemma 1 and Lemma 3 (taking for \(P\) a projective space of sufficiently large dimension \(r\), for example \(r > \dim X + 1\)). Q.E.D.

N.B. We have used the fact that \(\gamma^{p-1}(E)\) was of filtration \(\geq p-1\). One can prove this fact directly, without passing through the Grassmannians as in R.R.R. 2.16, by studying the bundle \(Y'\) of projective spaces associated with \(E\). This method gives, more generally, Th. 2.14 of R.R.R.
Extract from a letter of Grothendieck

...'morally', the new method rests on the determination of \(K(X)\) and of \(A(X)\) when \(X\) is either a projective bundle associated with a vector bundle, or a variety obtained by blowing up a nonsingular subvariety. In this second case, I have not carried out the complete determination; a little something is missing. With the notations of the little paper, one must prove formula (9) (but as an equality): \[f^* i_*(\alpha) = j_*(\alpha C^{p-1}(F)) , \quad \alpha \in A(Y) . \tag{9}\] One observes that the two members have the same image under \(f_*\), and the same image under \(j^*\), so that the question is equivalent to: \[\operatorname{Ker} f_* \cap \operatorname{Ker} j^* = 0 . \tag{9 bis}\] In any case, (9) is true after reducing to \(G(K(X'))\) (which destroys a bit of torsion, at most), for it then follows from (6); there is thus hardly any doubt that it is true.
... I point out to you, moreover, that the equality (9) follows very easily from (6) without passing through the tedious (6 bis)...
p.c.c. J-P.S.
